img.compress.s1.label: How it works
img.compress.s1.title: Compress images in three simple steps
img.compress.s1.body: Compressing images does not have to mean uploading your files to a distant server and hoping the connection is fast enough. This free online image compressor runs entirely inside your web browser, leveraging the HTML5 Canvas API and WebAssembly to deliver server-grade compression performance without any server at all. There is no desktop software to download, no browser extension to install, and no user account to register. Simply open the page, drop your images onto the upload zone, choose your quality and format settings, and watch as each file is compressed in real time — with live before-and-after file size comparisons updating as each image is processed. The entire workflow from upload to download typically takes under ten seconds for a batch of twenty images, and because nothing travels over the network beyond the initial page load, your bandwidth usage is effectively zero. This makes the tool ideal for mobile users on limited data plans, remote workers on satellite connections, and anyone who values both speed and privacy. The compression engine supports JPEG, PNG, WebP, GIF, BMP, and TIFF as input formats, giving you the flexibility to compress virtually any image file you encounter without first converting it to a supported type.

img.compress.s2.label: Smart compression
img.compress.s2.title: Reduce file size without sacrificing quality
img.compress.s2.body1: Our browser-side compression engine does not just apply a blanket quality reduction — it intelligently removes invisible data from your images while preserving the visual information that matters most to the human eye. JPEG files, for example, contain EXIF metadata blocks, ICC colour profile information, and embedded thumbnail previews that can account for tens of kilobytes of overhead before you even reach the pixel data. Our engine strips this non-essential data first, then applies the JPEG quantisation algorithm at your chosen quality level, optimising the Huffman coding tables for the smallest possible file representation. The result is a typical size reduction of 40 to 80 percent for JPEG files with virtually no perceptible quality loss when viewed on a screen at normal resolution. PNG files benefit from colour depth optimisation — if an image uses fewer than 256 distinct colours, the engine automatically converts the colour space from 24-bit RGB to an indexed palette, often cutting file size by more than half without any visual change whatsoever. For WebP files, the encoder leverages both lossy and lossless modes depending on the quality setting, delivering state-of-the-art compression that outperforms JPEG and PNG in most scenarios.
img.compress.s2.body2: The quality slider gives you fine-grained control over the compression trade-off, with a range from 1 to 100 that maps directly to the output quality parameter used by the browser's native image encoder. At the high end — quality 80 to 100 — the output is virtually indistinguishable from the original, making it safe for product photography, portfolio images, commercial prints, and any context where visual fidelity is paramount. In the middle range — quality 50 to 80 — you get meaningful file size reductions with only subtle differences that are imperceptible at normal viewing distances, which is ideal for blog posts, news articles, and social media content where perfect quality is not required but professional appearance matters. At the lower end — quality 1 to 50 — file sizes shrink dramatically, producing highly compressed images that are perfect for thumbnails, preview images, email attachments, and bandwidth-constrained mobile contexts where fast loading takes priority over pixel-perfect detail. Because the tool processes images locally, you can experiment with different quality settings in real time — compress at quality 80, check the result, then try 60 and compare — all without re-uploading or waiting for a server response. This iterative approach helps you find the optimal quality setting for each type of image content, and the per-image statistics showing original size, compressed size, and percentage reduction make it easy to evaluate the trade-off quantitatively.
img.compress.s3.label: Format support
img.compress.s3.title: Compress & convert every major format
img.compress.s3.body: Whether you are working with photographs, vector-style graphics, web assets, or scanned documents, this tool handles every major image format with format-specific optimisation strategies. JPEG compression is optimised for photographic content with smooth colour gradients, delivering dramatic size reductions by exploiting the inherent limitations of human visual perception — subtle colour variations that the eye cannot distinguish are merged, and high-frequency detail that is invisible at normal viewing distance is discarded. PNG compression targets lossless scenarios where every pixel must be preserved exactly — logos, screenshots, diagrams, and images with alpha-channel transparency — by optimising colour depth, scanline filtering, and deflate compression parameters to squeeze out maximum savings without altering a single pixel value. WebP, Google's modern image format, combines the lossy efficiency of JPEG with the transparency support of PNG, and our engine takes full advantage of WebP's advanced encoding options to produce files that are 25 to 35 percent smaller than equivalent-quality JPEGs. Beyond compression, the tool can convert between all supported formats in a single step — compress a PNG and output it as WebP for the web, or take a JPEG and save it as a PNG for lossless archival. You can also set an optional maximum width that automatically down-scales oversized images during compression, ensuring that your output files are not just smaller in bytes but also appropriately sized for their intended display context, whether that is a website hero image, an email template, or a social media post.
img.compress.s3.fmt.jpg: Best for photographs and complex images with many colors. Adjustable lossy compression keeps quality high while shrinking file size dramatically.
img.compress.s3.fmt.png: Lossless format ideal for graphics, logos, and images with transparency. We reduce PNG size by optimizing color depth and metadata.
img.compress.s3.fmt.webp: Google's modern format delivers 25–35% smaller files than JPEG at equivalent quality. Perfect for web pages that need fast load times.
img.compress.s3.fmt.resize.name: Resize on compress
img.compress.s3.fmt.resize.desc: Set a maximum width to automatically downscale oversized images. Aspect ratio is always preserved — no stretching or distortion.
img.compress.s4.label: 100% private
img.compress.s4.title: Your images never leave your browser
img.compress.s4.body1: In an era of increasing data breaches and privacy regulations, the way your images are processed matters as much as the compression results themselves. This tool was designed from the ground up with a zero-upload architecture: every pixel of every image you select is read, decoded, compressed, and re-encoded entirely within your browser's sandboxed JavaScript environment. No image data is ever sent to any server, whether ours or a third party's. There are no XMLHttpRequest or fetch calls carrying image data, no WebSocket connections streaming your files to a remote endpoint, and no hidden form submissions. When you close the browser tab, the images exist only in your device's temporary memory and are immediately released by the garbage collector — there is no local storage, no IndexedDB persistence, and no service-worker cache that retains your files. This architectural choice was deliberate and fundamental, not an afterthought, ensuring that the tool can be safely used for the most sensitive images imaginable: medical scans that are protected under HIPAA, legal document scans subject to attorney-client privilege, confidential business presentations containing trade secrets, personal photographs that should never leave your device, and any other content that should never be exposed to an external party.
img.compress.s4.body2: The privacy guarantee extends beyond the technical architecture to every aspect of the user experience. There is no account registration, so we have no email address, name, or profile to associate with your usage. There are no tracking cookies monitoring which pages you visit or which tools you use. There is no browser fingerprinting, no Google Analytics or Facebook Pixel recording your behaviour, and no advertising network collecting data about your activity. The tool does not ask for camera access, microphone access, or any browser permission beyond what is needed to display the page itself. For organisations subject to GDPR in Europe, CCPA in California, HIPAA in healthcare, or similar data-protection regulations around the world, this means there is no data controller relationship to establish, no data processing agreement to sign, and no risk of non-compliance arising from the use of this tool. You can compress images with complete confidence that your data stays yours. In a landscape where free online tools routinely monetize user uploads — selling anonymised data to advertisers, training machine learning models on user content, or quietly retaining files for undisclosed purposes — this zero-data approach stands in stark contrast.
img.compress.s5.label: Batch processing
img.compress.s5.title: Compress up to 20 images at once
img.compress.s5.body1: Batch compression transforms a time-consuming, repetitive chore into a single streamlined operation. Instead of uploading and compressing images one at a time — a process that can take hours when you have dozens or hundreds of files to process — you can drag and drop up to 20 images at once and let the tool process them all in parallel. The browser's multi-threaded WebAssembly engine divides the work across available CPU cores, so a batch of 20 images takes barely longer than compressing a single image. Each image in the batch displays its own live progress indicator, and as soon as compression completes, the before-and-after file sizes appear alongside the compression ratio and the exact number of bytes saved. This per-image feedback lets you quickly identify any outlier — an image that did not compress well because it was already heavily optimised, for instance — and decide whether to re-process it with different settings. When every image meets your expectations, the Download All as ZIP button packages all compressed files into a single archive, saving you from clicking download twenty separate times.
img.compress.s5.body2: Batch processing is essential in a wide range of real-world scenarios. Web developers preparing a site launch or migration often need to compress hundreds of images to meet Google's Core Web Vitals thresholds for Largest Contentful Paint and reduce overall page weight below recommended limits. Rather than running each image through a desktop application individually, they can batch-compress twenty images at a time and accumulate the results in seconds per batch. Digital marketers managing email campaigns need to keep attachment sizes under strict limits — typically 1 to 5 megabytes per email — and batch compression lets them compress an entire newsletter's worth of images at once, guaranteeing that the total stays within budget. E-commerce sellers maintaining product catalogues on platforms like Shopify, WooCommerce, Amazon, and eBay benefit from uniform compression that keeps their store's page load times fast and consistent across all product pages. Bloggers and content creators who publish image-heavy articles can batch-compress all illustrations before uploading to their CMS, preventing slow page loads that increase bounce rates and hurt search engine rankings. Social media managers preparing assets for multiple platforms can batch-compress images to different quality levels for each platform's requirements. In every case, the combination of batch processing, real-time feedback, and one-click ZIP download turns what was once a tedious multi-step process into a smooth, efficient workflow that saves hours of manual work every week.

img.compress.s6.title: The best free online image compressor — fast, private, and powerful
img.compress.s6.body1: This free online image compressor was designed to be the only image optimisation tool you ever need, whether you are a casual user shrinking a single photo for an email attachment or a professional web developer optimising hundreds of assets for a high-traffic website. It combines powerful compression capabilities with an unwavering commitment to privacy and a user interface so simple that anyone can use it without reading a manual. At its core, the tool reduces the file size of JPEG, PNG, WebP, GIF, and BMP images by applying intelligent compression algorithms directly in your browser using the Canvas API and WebAssembly. There is no upload step, no server queue, and no waiting for results to be emailed back to you. You drag and drop your images, adjust the quality slider to your preferred balance of file size versus visual quality, optionally choose a different output format or set a maximum width for automatic down-scaling, and then click Compress. Results appear instantly, with each image showing the original file size, the compressed file size, the compression ratio as a percentage, and the exact number of bytes saved. Download images one at a time or grab them all in a single ZIP archive with one click.
img.compress.s6.body2: What truly distinguishes this tool from alternatives like TinyPNG, Squoosh, Compressor.io, and other popular image compression services is its zero-upload architecture. Every competing tool in this space — even those that claim to respect your privacy — transmits your images to a remote server for processing. That transmission alone creates a privacy risk, regardless of how quickly the server claims to delete the file afterward. Data in transit can be intercepted, server logs can retain metadata about your uploads, and server-side breaches can expose files that were supposed to be temporary. Our tool eliminates that risk entirely by performing all compression locally using the browser's Canvas API and WebAssembly technology. Your images never leave your device, period. This makes the tool suitable for compressing confidential documents, sensitive medical images, proprietary design files, personal photographs, and any other content that should not be transmitted to a third party. The absence of a server also means there are no daily usage limits, no file-size quotas, and no premium tier to purchase. Every feature — batch processing, format conversion, quality adjustment, maximum width scaling, ZIP download — is available to every user, completely free of charge, with no registration required.
img.compress.s6.body3: Key features include: batch compression of up to 20 images simultaneously, with each image processed in parallel for maximum speed; an adjustable quality slider from 1 to 100 percent that gives you complete control over the file size versus visual fidelity trade-off; one-click format conversion between JPEG, PNG, and WebP, allowing you to compress and convert in a single step rather than chaining two separate tools; an optional maximum width setting that automatically down-scales oversized images while preserving aspect ratio, perfect for standardising a folder of mixed-resolution photos to a uniform display width; real-time per-image statistics showing original size, compressed size, compression ratio, and bytes saved; a Download All as ZIP button that packages every compressed image into a single archive for convenient bulk download; and full browser-side processing that ensures your images never leave your device. The tool supports JPEG, PNG, WebP, GIF, BMP, and TIFF as input formats, and JPEG, PNG, and WebP as output formats. It works on all modern browsers including Chrome, Firefox, Safari, and Edge on desktop, as well as Chrome for Android and Safari for iOS. The responsive interface adapts to both desktop monitors and mobile phone screens, so you can compress images on your phone while commuting just as easily as on your workstation. Start compressing your images for free right now — just drag, drop, and download.

img.resize.about.title: The most powerful free image resizer — resize JPG, PNG, WebP online
img.resize.about.body1: Need to resize images for a website, social media campaign, email newsletter, or print project? This free online image resizer handles every scenario with professional precision — directly in your browser, with no software to install and no account required. Simply drag and drop your JPG, PNG, or WebP files, set your target dimensions, and download the resized images in seconds. The tool supports three distinct resizing modes — Pixel mode for exact dimension input, Percentage mode for proportional scaling, and Preset mode with twelve built-in social media templates — giving you the flexibility to handle any resizing task without switching between different applications. Whether you are an occasional user who needs to shrink a photo for an email attachment or a professional designer preparing dozens of assets for a multi-platform campaign, the workflow is identical: upload, configure, download. There are no hidden paywalls, no watermarked outputs, and no artificial limits on file count or image resolution.
img.resize.about.body2: Three resize modes give you complete flexibility. In Pixel mode, enter exact width and height values from 1 to 10,000 pixels — perfect when you need a specific canvas size for a web banner or print template. This mode supports independent width and height entry as well as a linked aspect ratio mode that automatically calculates the corresponding dimension when you change one value. In Percentage mode, scale images from 10% to 200% of their original size with a smooth slider, ideal for quick size reductions or upscaling tests without needing to know the original pixel dimensions. In Preset mode, choose from 12 built-in social media templates: Instagram Post (1080x1080), Instagram Story (1080x1920), Facebook Cover (820x312), YouTube Thumbnail (1280x720), Twitter/X Post (1200x675), LinkedIn Post (1200x627), Full HD (1920x1080), 4K (3840x2160), and more — all updated to match each platform's current recommended dimensions. Selecting a preset instantly populates the width and height fields, and if you need a custom size that is not covered by a template, you can always override the values manually after selecting the closest preset as a starting point.
img.resize.about.body3: The aspect ratio lock prevents unwanted distortion: enable it and changing one dimension automatically updates the other to maintain the original proportions. Disable it to set completely independent width and height values for creative crops or when fitting an image into a non-standard container. After resizing, choose your output format — keep the original, convert to JPG for maximum web compatibility and the smallest file sizes, switch to PNG for lossless quality and transparency support, or pick WebP for the optimal balance between file size and visual fidelity. A quality slider (20-100%) lets you fine-tune the file size vs. visual quality trade-off for JPG and WebP outputs. Higher quality settings preserve more detail at the cost of larger files, while lower settings dramatically reduce file size with only subtle visible differences, especially on screens where the image is displayed at a small size. This makes it easy to find the sweet spot for each use case — high quality for print and portfolio work, medium for general web use, and aggressive compression for mobile-optimised pages where bandwidth savings are paramount.
img.resize.about.body4: The tool is powered entirely by the browser's Canvas API, which means all processing happens locally on your device — no files are ever transmitted to a server, eliminating both upload waiting time and privacy concerns. This local-first architecture delivers several practical advantages beyond privacy. First, speed: because there is no round-trip to a server, resizing is nearly instantaneous. A typical 5-megapixel JPEG resizes in under 200 milliseconds on modern hardware, and even large batches of 20 images complete in just a few seconds. Second, reliability: the tool works offline once the page has loaded, so you can resize images on a laptop during a flight or in a coffee shop with spotty Wi-Fi without any interruption. Third, transparency: because the code runs in your browser, a technically inclined user can open the browser's developer tools and inspect exactly what the JavaScript is doing — there are no hidden server-side operations that could behave unexpectedly. This makes it safe to resize confidential business images, personal photos, or sensitive documents without worry.
img.resize.about.body5: Whether you are a web developer optimising page load speeds by serving correctly sized images instead of relying on CSS scaling, a social media manager preparing platform-specific assets for Instagram, Facebook, Twitter, LinkedIn, and YouTube simultaneously, an e-commerce seller standardising product photos to meet marketplace requirements on Amazon, eBay, and Shopify, or a designer prepping files for a client presentation — this tool saves hours of repetitive work every week. It works on Windows, Mac, iOS, Android and any modern browser including Chrome, Firefox, Safari, and Edge. No plugins, no subscriptions, no watermarks on output. The responsive interface adapts to both desktop and mobile screens, so you can resize images on your phone while commuting just as easily as on your workstation. Just drag, set your dimensions, and download. Try it once and you will wonder why you ever used a server-based tool for something this simple.

img.resize.s5.body1: Batch processing transforms a tedious, repetitive task into a single streamlined operation. Instead of resizing images one at a time — opening each file, typing dimensions, waiting for output, downloading, and repeating — you can drag and drop up to 20 images at once and apply the same resize settings to every one of them simultaneously. The tool processes all images in parallel using your browser's native Canvas rendering pipeline, so the total time is barely longer than resizing a single file. Once processing is complete, you can review each result individually, comparing the original and resized dimensions side by side, before downloading. If you need different settings for certain images, simply adjust and re-process — the originals remain in memory so you never need to re-upload. When you are satisfied with every result, click the Download All button to receive a single ZIP archive containing all resized images, neatly organized and ready to deploy.
img.resize.s5.body2: Batch resizing is indispensable in a wide range of professional scenarios. Web developers managing a CMS with strict image dimension requirements can prepare an entire page's worth of assets in one batch. Social media managers who need to post the same image across multiple platforms — each with its own recommended dimensions — can generate all required sizes in a single pass. E-commerce sellers maintaining product catalogues with hundreds of SKUs can enforce uniform image dimensions across their entire inventory, ensuring a consistent grid layout on category pages and search results. Bloggers compiling image-heavy articles can resize all illustrations to a uniform width before uploading, preventing layout shifts and improving page speed scores. Photographers delivering web-resolution proofs to clients can batch-resize an entire shoot folder to a tasteful display size, protecting full-resolution originals while still providing useful previews. In every case, the ZIP download saves minutes of individual file-saving clicks, and the real-time per-image statistics — showing original and new dimensions plus the percentage change — let you verify results at a glance.
img.resize.s6.label: About this tool
img.resize.s6.title: The best free online image resizer — precise, private, and powerful
img.resize.s6.body1: This free online image resizer was built to be the fastest and most privacy-respecting way to change image dimensions on the web. It supports three flexible resize modes — exact pixels, percentage scaling, and social media presets — so you can choose the approach that best fits your workflow. In pixel mode, enter any width and height from 1 to 10,000 pixels and the tool will produce output that matches those dimensions to the dot. In percentage mode, a smooth slider lets you scale images from 10 percent to 200 percent of their original size, which is ideal when you know you want something roughly half or roughly double the current size but do not need a specific pixel count. In preset mode, 12 built-in templates for major social media and display platforms fill in the correct dimensions automatically, saving you the trouble of looking up each platform's specifications. All three modes support the aspect ratio lock, format conversion between JPEG, PNG, and WebP, and a quality slider that fine-tunes the balance between visual fidelity and file size.
img.resize.s6.body2: The technical design of the tool sets it apart from alternatives. Rather than sending your images to a remote server for processing — which consumes upload bandwidth, introduces latency, and creates privacy concerns — every resize operation executes locally in your browser using the Canvas API. This client-side architecture means results appear almost instantly for typical web-resolution images, and even multi-megapixel photos are processed in well under a second on modern hardware. There is no server queue to wait in, no daily usage cap, and no premium tier required to unlock batch processing or format conversion. Every feature is available to every user, free of charge, with no strings attached. The tool works on any modern browser including Chrome, Firefox, Safari, and Edge on desktop, as well as Chrome for Android and Safari for iOS, so you can resize images on your phone, tablet, or laptop with identical functionality.
img.resize.s6.body3: Who benefits most from this tool? Web developers who need to optimize page speed by serving correctly sized images instead of relying on browser-side scaling, which wastes bandwidth and slows paint times. Social media managers who juggle assets across Instagram, Facebook, Twitter, LinkedIn, YouTube, and Pinterest — each with unique dimension requirements that change periodically. E-commerce sellers on Shopify, WooCommerce, Amazon, and eBay who must match strict image dimension guidelines to avoid rejected listings or inconsistent storefronts. Graphic designers who need to quickly generate resized variants of a master asset for different deliverables. Bloggers and content creators who want to standardise featured image dimensions for a clean, professional layout. Photographers who deliver web-resolution proofs to clients while keeping full-resolution originals for final delivery. Students and educators who need to resize images for presentations, reports, and learning management systems with file-size limits. In short, anyone who works with digital images will find this tool saves time, protects privacy, and delivers professional results with zero friction.

img.metadata.about.label: About
img.metadata.about.title: Read hidden EXIF, GPS, IPTC and XMP data from any image — free online
img.metadata.about.body1: Every digital photograph contains a rich layer of invisible metadata that tells the complete story behind the image: the specific camera body and lens used to capture it, the exact exposure settings chosen by the photographer, the precise date and time the shutter was pressed, and — in the case of most smartphones — the geographic coordinates of the location where the photo was taken. This free online image metadata viewer extracts all of that information and more, parsing EXIF, GPS, IPTC, and XMP data structures directly in your browser from JPEG, PNG, WebP, TIFF, and HEIC files. No image data is ever uploaded to any external server. The tool uses the exifr.js library, a high-performance JavaScript EXIF parser that runs entirely client-side, to read binary metadata blocks from your images without needing to decode the full pixel data. This means even very large image files — up to 20 megabytes each — are parsed almost instantly, typically in under a second. Upload up to 10 images simultaneously and switch between them using the sidebar list to compare metadata across photos from the same shoot, photos taken with different cameras, or images from different time periods that you want to analyse side by side. The metadata is presented in six clearly labelled categories — Basic Info, Camera Settings, GPS Location, IPTC Copyright, XMP Data, and Raw Data — so you can jump directly to the section you need without scrolling through irrelevant fields. Each field displays both the technical tag name and a human-readable description, making the tool accessible to both technical users who want raw EXIF tag names and non-technical users who simply want to understand what information their photos reveal about them. Whether you are verifying your own camera settings, checking an image for hidden GPS data before sharing it online, or auditing a large collection of photos for consistent metadata, this tool provides everything you need in a single, fast, and completely private interface.
img.metadata.about.body2: For photographers and photo archivists, EXIF data is an invaluable resource that reveals the complete technical story behind every shot. The parsed data includes the camera manufacturer and model, the lens make and model, ISO sensitivity, aperture value expressed as an f-number, shutter speed in seconds or fractions of a second, focal length in millimetres, flash firing status and mode, white balance setting, metering mode, exposure compensation value, and the exact date and time the photograph was captured down to the second. Some professional cameras also embed serial numbers for both the body and the lens, which is essential for tracking equipment usage across thousands of images in a professional workflow and for insurance documentation in case of theft or loss. This information is critical during photo culling sessions, where reviewing the camera settings of your best shots helps you understand which combinations of aperture, shutter speed, and ISO produce the results you want in specific lighting conditions. Over time, analysing EXIF data across your entire portfolio builds a data-driven understanding of your shooting habits — your most-used focal lengths, your typical ISO ranges, your preferred aperture settings — helping you make more deliberate creative choices and select the right gear for each situation. For stock photographers contributing to agencies like Shutterstock, Adobe Stock, or Getty Images, accurate EXIF data is often a submission requirement that must be present and correct before an image is accepted into the library. For photo archivists managing historical collections, EXIF timestamps and camera models help establish provenance and approximate dates for undated photographs, supporting cataloguing efforts and scholarly research. Photography students and educators also benefit from studying EXIF data to understand how different exposure settings affect the final image, providing real-world examples that complement theoretical instruction.
img.metadata.about.body3: For privacy-conscious users, journalists, activists, and anyone who shares photos online, GPS coordinates embedded in smartphone images represent a significant and often overlooked privacy risk. Every time you take a photo with location services enabled on your phone, the device embeds the precise latitude, longitude, and sometimes altitude into the image's EXIF GPS tags using data from the phone's GPS receiver, Wi-Fi positioning system, and cellular triangulation. This means that a single photo shared on social media, sent in an email attachment, or posted to a public forum can reveal exactly where you were when you took it — your home address, your workplace, a friend's house, a medical facility, a child's school, a hotel room, or any other sensitive location. Research has shown that automated tools can extract GPS coordinates from images posted online within seconds, enabling stalking, harassment, and targeted attacks. This tool lets you inspect the GPS data in your images before you share them, displaying the coordinates numerically with full decimal precision and plotting them on an interactive OpenStreetMap with a pin marking the exact shooting location. A direct link lets you open the location in your preferred maps application for a closer look. By making hidden location data visible, the tool empowers you to make informed decisions about which images are safe to share and which need their metadata stripped first. Journalists working in sensitive regions, activists organising protests or meetings, domestic violence survivors who have relocated, and anyone concerned about digital stalking or location tracking will find this capability essential for their personal safety and operational security. We recommend inspecting GPS metadata in every photo before uploading it to any public platform, social media site, or shared album.
img.metadata.about.body4: IPTC metadata is the professional standard for embedding copyright and editorial information directly inside image files. Originally developed by the International Press Telecommunications Council for the news wire industry in the early 1990s, IPTC fields are now used worldwide by photo agencies, stock libraries, publishers, and digital asset management systems to track ownership, licensing terms, and editorial context. Our tool reads IPTC fields including the creator's name, copyright notice, image title, description or caption, keywords, city, sub-location, province or state, country, and special instructions governing image usage and restrictions. Stock photographers rely on IPTC data to ensure their images are properly attributed wherever they are published online or in print, while photo editors at news organisations use IPTC captions and keywords to search and categorise incoming wire images at speed during breaking news events. XMP data, originally developed by Adobe as an extensible metadata framework based on RDF and XML, carries additional workflow information that IPTC alone cannot represent. This includes star ratings, colour labels applied in Lightroom or Capture One, the names and versions of editing software used to process the image, the colour space (sRGB, Adobe RGB, ProPhoto RGB), and detailed editing history that records every adjustment made to the file from import to final export. For professionals working across multiple applications in a photography or design workflow, XMP metadata is the thread that maintains consistency and context as files move between tools, ensuring that a five-star rating applied in Lightroom is still visible when the image is opened in Bridge, Capture One, or any other XMP-aware application. Understanding the metadata embedded in your images is the first step toward building a robust digital asset management strategy that protects your copyright and streamlines your creative workflow.
img.metadata.about.body5: The tool supports batch processing of up to 10 images simultaneously, with a sidebar list that lets you switch between images instantly to compare their metadata side by side. This is particularly useful when auditing a photo library for consistent camera settings across a shoot, checking that all images from an event carry the same copyright information, or verifying that GPS data is present in some images but absent in others — a common situation when location services were accidentally enabled for some photos but not others. Export options are designed to match the needs of different audiences and workflows. The JSON export produces structured, machine-readable data that developers can integrate directly into applications, scripts, or CI/CD pipelines — perfect for building automated metadata analysis tools, image cataloguing systems, or quality-assurance checks that flag images missing required metadata fields. The CSV export formats metadata as a spreadsheet, allowing non-technical users to sort, filter, and analyse image information in Excel, Google Sheets, or Apple Numbers without writing any code. The TXT export generates a human-readable report that can be shared in an email, pasted into a document, or printed for archival purposes and legal documentation. All exports can be downloaded individually or packaged into a single ZIP archive for convenient bulk download when working with multiple images. Because every byte of processing happens locally using the exifr.js library, your images — including sensitive GPS coordinates, personal information, and proprietary copyright data — never leave your device. No account, no tracking, no server, no compromise. Start viewing your image metadata right now — simply drag, drop, and explore all the hidden data inside your photos.

img.metadata.privacy.label: 100% Private
img.metadata.privacy.title: Your images never leave your browser
img.metadata.privacy.body1: All metadata parsing in this tool is performed locally using the exifr.js JavaScript library, which runs entirely inside your browser's sandboxed execution environment. When you select an image file, the browser reads its binary data into an ArrayBuffer in memory, and exifr.js parses the EXIF, IPTC, GPS, and XMP metadata blocks directly from that buffer without ever transmitting a single byte over the network. Your images — including sensitive GPS coordinates that could reveal your home address, personal copyright information that identifies you by name, and camera serial numbers that link the image to specific equipment you own — are never uploaded to any server. There is no WebSocket connection, no XMLHttpRequest carrying image data, and no server-side API call involved at any point in the process. The tool does not use WebRTC, which could potentially leak IP addresses through STUN servers. It does not request camera, microphone, or geolocation permissions from your browser. When you close the tab, all image data held in JavaScript memory is released to the browser's garbage collector and cannot be recovered — there is no persistent storage of any kind. This zero-transmission architecture ensures that the tool can be safely used for the most sensitive images in existence: legal document scans subject to attorney-client privilege, medical photographs protected under HIPAA, confidential business presentations containing trade secrets, journalistic source material that could identify a whistleblower, and personal photographs that should never be exposed to any external party under any circumstances. You can verify these claims yourself by opening your browser's developer tools Network tab and confirming that no image data is transmitted during the entire metadata extraction process.
img.metadata.privacy.body2: Beyond the network-level privacy guarantee, the tool respects your privacy in every other dimension as well. There is no registration form and no account system, so we have no way to identify you — no email address, no username, no social login, no IP address logged in a database. There are no tracking cookies placed on your device, no Google Analytics or Facebook Pixel recording which pages you visit or which features you use, and no browser fingerprinting technique used to follow you across sessions. The tool does not store your images or their metadata in localStorage, IndexedDB, or any other persistent browser storage mechanism. It does not record which images you viewed, which metadata fields you inspected, which map locations you explored, or which export formats you used. For organisations subject to GDPR in Europe, CCPA in California, HIPAA in healthcare, or similar data protection regulations around the world, this means there is no personal data being processed, no data controller relationship to establish, no data processing agreement to sign, and no compliance paperwork of any kind required. The tool can be deployed in corporate environments, government agencies, healthcare facilities, and legal offices without triggering any procurement or compliance review processes. Inspect confidential photos, verify metadata before sharing images publicly, audit your entire photo library for GPS data leaks, and generate export reports — all with complete confidence that your activity is invisible to everyone, including us. Open the tool, view your metadata, close the tab — that is the entire lifecycle, with no digital footprint left behind.

ocr.s1.label: What is OCR
ocr.s1.title: Extract text from images with AI-powered OCR technology
ocr.s2.label: How it works
ocr.s2.title: Powerful OCR engine with 25+ language support
ocr.s2.body1: Under the hood, the process starts the moment your image reaches the recognition engine. Before any actual character recognition happens, the engine runs through a series of preprocessing steps that make a big difference in accuracy. It checks whether the image is rotated or tilted — a common issue with phone photos — and corrects the orientation automatically. It scales the image to a resolution that gives the neural network enough pixel detail to work with, even if your original photo was low-resolution. Then it starts the real work: scanning the image for regions that look like text, breaking those regions into lines, breaking lines into words, and breaking words into individual characters. Engine 2, which runs by default, uses a modern convolutional neural network that has been trained on a vastly larger and more diverse dataset than the older Engine 1. The difference shows most clearly on difficult inputs — photos taken at awkward angles, documents with non-standard layouts, faded prints, or images where the text sits on top of a colored or textured background. For documents with mixed content — say, a page that contains both a table of numerical data and several paragraphs of explanatory text — Engine 2's ability to maintain structural context across different regions of the page produces noticeably better results than attempting to recognize each text block in isolation. The preprocessing pipeline also handles image sharpening and contrast enhancement automatically, which helps rescue text from slightly blurry photographs without requiring you to run your images through a separate image editing tool first.
ocr.s2.body2: Once the engine finishes its analysis, you get two things back: the extracted text itself and a confidence score. The confidence score deserves a brief explanation because it actually tells you something useful. A score above 90 percent means the engine is quite sure about what it read — the source image was probably clear, well-lit, and used a standard font. Scores in the 70 to 90 range are still perfectly usable but suggest you should skim the output for small errors — maybe a lowercase "l" was confused with an uppercase "I," or an "rn" was read as an "m." Below 70 percent, you'll want to read through the text more carefully and expect to make corrections. The output area is a regular text editor, so you can fix mistakes right there. Word count and character count update as you type, which is handy if you're trying to hit a specific length. When you're satisfied with the text, copy it to your clipboard or download it as a plain text file.
ocr.s3.label: Privacy first
ocr.s3.title: Your images are processed securely and never stored
ocr.s4.label: Features
ocr.s4.title: Everything you need for text extraction
ocr.s4.body1: Language support is one of those things that sounds simple but gets complicated fast. This tool handles over 25 languages, and for most of them, you'll get better results if you explicitly select the language from the dropdown before running recognition. The reason is straightforward: the engine loads a different recognition model depending on the language, and each model is optimized for the specific character set, common ligatures, and typographic conventions of that language. English and other Latin-script languages share enough structural similarity that the engine can often handle them without explicit language selection. Chinese, Japanese, and Korean are different stories entirely — they use completely different character sets with thousands of unique glyphs, and the engine needs to know which one to activate. The good news is that Engine 2 can often handle documents where two or three languages appear on the same page, which happens more often than you might think in business documents, academic papers, and bilingual signage. If you regularly work with documents in a particular language, selecting that language becomes second nature and takes a fraction of a second. The language dropdown also serves as a signal to the engine about which character set to prioritize when ambiguous shapes appear — for example, the distinction between the letter "O" and the digit "0", or between a lowercase "l", an uppercase "I", and the numeral "1", which varies across languages and typographic traditions.
ocr.s4.body2: You might notice two engine options in the settings panel. Engine 2 is the one you should use for almost everything. It's slower than Engine 1 by a couple of seconds, but the accuracy improvement is substantial — especially on the kind of imperfect images that most people actually upload, as opposed to the clean test images that look great in marketing demos. Engine 1 is faster and lighter, which makes it a reasonable choice if you're processing a very straightforward document with standard fonts and good image quality, or if you're in a hurry and want a quick first pass. The auto-orientation toggle is worth leaving on. It tells the engine to figure out which way is "up" in your image, which solves the surprisingly common problem of photos that were taken at an angle or documents that were scanned upside down.
ocr.s4.body3: After recognition completes, you'll see the extracted text in an editable area alongside a color-coded confidence bar. Green means high confidence — the engine read the text cleanly. Amber means moderate confidence — most of the text is probably correct, but double-check numbers, proper nouns, and anything in an unusual font. Red means the engine struggled, usually because the image was blurry, very small, or had text running over a complex background. The text area itself works like any basic text editor. You can select, delete, retype, and add line breaks. The word and character counts update live as you edit, which is useful if you're trying to stay within a character limit for a form, a social media post, or a database field. When you're done, hit the copy button to send the text to your clipboard, or download it as a .txt file. The combination of editable output, live statistics, and visual confidence feedback creates a workflow that's transparent at every step. You always know how well the recognition performed, how much text was extracted, and what the output looks like before you commit to copying or downloading it. There are no hidden steps or surprise results.
ocr.s5.label: Use cases
ocr.s5.title: Perfect for students, professionals, and researchers
ocr.s5.body1: The practical applications of OCR extend far beyond what most people initially imagine. Consider academic research: a graduate student working on a literature review might photograph relevant pages from a library book that can't be checked out. OCR converts those photos into searchable, quotable text in seconds, without the tedious manual transcription that used to consume hours of research time. In legal practice, attorneys regularly receive scanned documents — contracts, correspondence, court filings — that need to be searched for specific terms, compared across versions, or excerpted for briefs. OCR turns those static scans into editable text that integrates smoothly into existing digital workflows. Medical professionals face a similar challenge: patient intake forms, prescription labels, referral letters, and insurance documents often exist only on paper. Converting them to digital text makes them accessible within electronic health record systems, reduces transcription errors, and speeds up the entire documentation process. Insurance companies use OCR to process claims forms and accident reports that arrive as scanned images or smartphone photos from policyholders. Government agencies digitize paper applications, permits, and correspondence to build searchable digital archives that reduce physical storage requirements and speed up response times for public records requests. Publishing houses use OCR to create digital editions of out-of-print books by scanning physical copies and converting the page images into editable manuscript files.
ocr.s5.body2: In the business world, the applications are equally broad and arguably more high-volume. Accounts payable teams use OCR to extract vendor names, invoice numbers, line items, and payment amounts from scanned invoices and receipts — data that then flows into accounting software without manual keying. Sales teams capture business card information at trade shows and conferences, converting a stack of cards into a spreadsheet of contact details before the event is even over. Content creators and journalists photograph whiteboards, posters, street signs, and product packaging, then use OCR to grab the text for articles, social media posts, or translation. International travelers find OCR particularly useful when combined with translation tools: photograph a menu in Tokyo, extract the Japanese text, paste it into a translator, and you can order dinner with confidence. The combination of OCR plus machine translation has made navigating foreign-language environments dramatically easier. Archivists and librarians use OCR to build searchable digital collections from historical documents, newspapers, and manuscripts that exist only in physical form, making previously inaccessible information available to researchers worldwide. The accessibility implications are significant too — OCR enables screen readers to narrate text that was previously locked inside image files, making visual content accessible to people with vision impairments who rely on assistive technology to navigate digital information.
ocr.s6.label: About this tool
ocr.s6.title: Best free online OCR tool — accurate, fast, private
ocr.s6.body1: The interface was designed around a simple observation: people who need to extract text from an image usually want to do it as quickly as possible and move on to their actual work. The two-panel layout reflects this. The left panel handles input — uploading or pasting an image, choosing the language, selecting the engine. The right panel handles output — reading the extracted text, making corrections, copying or downloading. Having both visible at the same time means you can glance at the original image while reviewing the recognized text, which makes it much easier to spot errors. There's no multi-step wizard, no configuration screen, no modal dialogs. Upload, recognize, edit, export. The entire workflow fits in a single screen. The two-panel layout also means the tool works well on wider screens where you can see both panels simultaneously, and on narrower screens where they stack vertically. On a laptop, the image preview and text output are both visible without scrolling, which makes the review-and-correct cycle fast and frictionless.
ocr.s6.body2: Image input works the way you'd expect from any modern web tool. You can click the upload button and pick a file from your device. You can drag and drop an image onto the upload zone. Or you can paste an image directly from your clipboard — this is especially useful for screenshots. If you've just pressed Print Screen or used your operating system's screenshot tool to capture an error message, a snippet of a web page, or a chat conversation, you can paste it directly into the tool with Ctrl+V (or Command+V on Mac) without saving it as a file first. Supported formats include JPG, PNG, WebP, BMP, TIFF, and GIF, with a maximum size of 5 MB per image — more than enough for high-resolution phone photos and detailed document scans.
ocr.s6.body3: To sum up: this tool takes an image, extracts the text from it, and puts that text in an editor where you can correct it, copy it, or download it. It supports over 25 languages, offers two engine options for different speed-and-accuracy tradeoffs, processes images in a few seconds, and deletes your data immediately after processing. Whether you're digitizing a book page, reading a receipt, converting a scanned form, or extracting text from a screenshot, the workflow is the same: upload, recognize, edit, and export. No accounts, no subscriptions, no software to install, and no data left behind on any server when you close the tab.

itv.s2.label: Transitions
itv.s2.title: Professional transition effects for cinematic slideshows
itv.s2.body1: Transitions are the difference between a video that feels intentional and one that feels like someone hit the right-arrow key really fast. The fade transition is probably the most versatile option here — it cross-dissolves from one image to the next by gradually changing opacity. The result is a soft, flowing visual effect that works well for nearly any type of content: photo galleries, event recaps, memorial slideshows, product showcases. It's the default for a reason. The slide transition takes a more directional approach: the current image moves off-screen while the next one slides in from the opposite side. This creates a sense of forward momentum that works especially well for sequences where order matters — a step-by-step tutorial, a before-and-after comparison, or a travelogue that follows a geographic path. The fade transition also serves as a visual "breathing room" between images that gives viewers a moment to process what they just saw before the next image appears. This psychological pacing effect is subtle but noticeable — videos without any transition can feel rushed and overwhelming, especially when images are displayed for only a second or two. A half-second fade adds just enough pause to make the viewing experience feel comfortable without significantly lengthening the total video duration.
itv.s2.body2: The zoom transition scales the current image up while fading in the next one underneath it. It draws the eye toward the center of the frame and creates a subtle sense of depth, as if the camera were pushing through the image toward the next scene. It's a good choice for highlight moments or dramatic reveals within a longer sequence. The "none" option skips transitions entirely and just cuts straight from one image to the next. This isn't a compromise — for certain types of content, hard cuts are actually the right choice. Timelapse sequences, stop-motion animation, scientific frame-by-frame documentation, and fast-paced social media clips all benefit from the immediacy of a direct cut. You can also adjust the transition duration from 0.2 seconds for quick blends up to 2 seconds for slow, dramatic crossfades. The right duration depends heavily on your content and where the video will be shown. The key is matching the transition style to your content's purpose and audience. A formal business presentation benefits from subtle fades, while a high-energy social media clip might call for quick zooms or hard cuts. There's no universally correct choice — it depends on the story you're telling and the impression you want to leave.
itv.s3.label: Quality control
itv.s3.title: Full control over resolution, frame rate, and output format
itv.s4.label: Easy to use
itv.s4.title: Drag, drop, reorder — create your video in minutes
itv.s4.body1: Getting images into the tool is straightforward. Click the Choose Images button, drag files from your desktop onto the upload zone, or select multiple files at once using your operating system's standard multi-file picker. Once uploaded, images appear as numbered thumbnails in a grid. Drag any thumbnail to reorder it — the number updates to reflect the new position. You can add more images later with the Add More button, remove individual images by hovering over them and clicking the X, or clear everything and start fresh. The tool handles up to 50 images per video. Each image's display duration can be set anywhere from half a second to ten seconds. In practice, most people settle on two to four seconds per image for social media content and five to eight seconds for presentation-style videos where the audience needs time to read text or absorb details. The grid interface provides visual feedback as you work: a numbered badge on each thumbnail shows its position in the sequence, and thumbnails display a scaled preview of the actual image content. This combination of visual preview and positional numbering makes it easy to verify at a glance that your sequence is correct before committing to a render. If you notice an image in the wrong position, a quick drag fixes it — no need to delete and re-upload.
itv.s4.body2: Before you commit to generating the full video, the built-in preview gives you a chance to verify everything looks right. The preview canvas in the settings panel plays through your image sequence with the selected transition effects and timing applied, at a reduced resolution for speed. It's not pixel-perfect to the final output, but it accurately represents the order, transitions, and pacing. The preview slideshow button cycles through all images sequentially so you can watch the flow from start to finish. If something looks off — an image in the wrong position, a transition that feels too slow, a duration that's too long — you can adjust the settings and preview again without waiting for a full render. When you're satisfied, hit the Generate Video button. A progress bar tracks the rendering in real time. For a typical 10-image slideshow at 720p with fade transitions, the whole process usually finishes in under ten seconds. If you want to experiment with different settings — maybe try a different transition style or adjust the duration — you can regenerate as many times as you like. Each generation overwrites the previous output, so there's no accumulation of files to clean up. This iterative approach to video creation is one of the advantages of local processing: no server quotas, no per-render limits, and no waiting for upload slots to become available.
itv.s5.label: Use cases
itv.s5.title: Perfect for presentations, social media, and memories
itv.s5.body1: The range of situations where image-to-video conversion comes in handy is remarkably broad. Real estate agents routinely turn property photos into tour videos that buyers can watch on their phones. E-commerce sellers create product videos from multiple angle shots — far more engaging than a static grid of thumbnails. Event photographers compile highlight reels from weddings, graduations, and corporate events. Social media managers build carousel videos optimized for Instagram Reels, TikTok, and YouTube Shorts, where smooth transitions and proper timing can make the difference between someone scrolling past and someone stopping to watch. Teachers and corporate trainers create instructional sequences from diagrams, charts, and step-by-step screenshots. Podcast creators and YouTubers sometimes convert audiogram-style images — static frames with waveform graphics overlaid on background artwork — into video files that can be uploaded to platforms that require video format. Small business owners create promotional videos from product catalog images and seasonal sale announcements. Conference speakers compile their slide decks into video format for sharing with attendees who couldn't attend in person.
itv.s5.body2: Beyond the obvious commercial and social media use cases, there are some less visible but equally important applications. Scientific researchers document experiments and environmental changes through time-delayed photography, then convert those image sequences into timelapse videos for papers, presentations, and public outreach. Construction firms photograph building progress daily or weekly, then compile those photos into project documentation videos for stakeholders and regulatory filings. Graphic designers and artists assemble portfolio videos that dynamically showcase their work across projects and media. Nonprofit organizations create awareness videos from field photography. Even hobbyists benefit — birdwatchers compile seasonal sighting sequences, gardeners document plant growth over months, and amateur astronomers stack time-series captures into animated visualizations. Personal users find plenty of occasions to convert images to video as well: creating anniversary and birthday montage videos from family photo collections, documenting home renovation progress over weeks or months, compiling vacation highlights into shareable clips, and creating memorial tribute videos that honor a loved one's memory with meaningful photographs set to thoughtful pacing.
itv.s6.label: About this tool
itv.s6.title: Best free online image to video converter — private, fast, no limits
itv.s6.body1: The technical architecture of this tool is worth understanding if you care about privacy, speed, or reliability. When you use a cloud-based video tool, your images travel across the internet to a remote server, get processed according to that server's queue and resource availability, and then the finished video travels back to you. That round trip introduces latency, potential privacy concerns, and dependency on the service provider's uptime and bandwidth. This tool takes a fundamentally different approach. The HTML5 Canvas API provides a programmable drawing surface where each image gets rendered at the target resolution. Transition effects are computed frame by frame through direct pixel manipulation on the canvas. The MediaRecorder API captures the canvas output stream, encodes it in real time at your selected frame rate, and produces a video file — all within the browser's sandbox on your own machine. An important consequence of this architecture is reliability. Because the tool doesn't depend on any external server for its core functionality, it works consistently regardless of server load, network conditions, or service availability. Once the page has loaded in your browser and the JavaScript has initialized, you can generate videos even if your internet connection drops — all the computation happens locally using your device's CPU and GPU resources.
itv.s6.body2: This local-first approach has concrete benefits. Processing starts the instant you click Generate — no upload time, no queue position to wait for. Your images never leave your device, which means there's no server-side copy, no temporary cache on someone else's infrastructure, and no possibility of unauthorized access to your creative assets. There are no file size caps or duration limits imposed by someone else's cloud infrastructure budget. The browser's rendering engine automatically takes advantage of GPU hardware acceleration where available, which means encoding speed on a modern laptop or desktop often matches what you'd get from dedicated desktop software. The trade-off is that very long, high-resolution videos with complex transitions may take slightly longer on older or less powerful hardware — but for the typical 10 to 30 image slideshow at 720p or 1080p, you're looking at seconds, not minutes. For users who work on laptops or older desktop hardware, the rendering time is still reasonable — a 20-image slideshow at 720p with fade transitions typically renders in under 30 seconds even on a five-year-old machine. The rendering process is also non-blocking in the sense that you can switch to another browser tab while it works, though the progress bar won't update until you return to the tool's tab.
itv.s6.body3: In practical terms: upload your images, drag them into the right order, pick a transition style and duration, choose your resolution and frame rate, preview the result, and hit Generate. Download the finished video. The whole process from first upload to final download typically takes under a minute for a standard slideshow. No software to install, no account to create, no subscription to manage. Your images stay on your device, the video is rendered locally, and nothing is transmitted to or stored on any server. Whether it's a quick birthday slideshow, a real estate property tour, an educational sequence, or a social media clip, the tool handles the technical work so you can focus on the creative decisions. The tool handles the technical complexity of video encoding so you can focus entirely on the creative decisions: which images to include, what order to present them in, how long each should linger on screen, and what transition style best fits the mood and purpose of your video. These are the decisions that actually determine whether your video resonates with viewers — the rendering engine just needs to faithfully execute your vision.

ipdf.s2.label: Page control
ipdf.s2.title: Full control over page size, orientation, and margins
ipdf.s2.body1: Page layout is where this tool gives you the most control, and it's worth taking a moment to understand the options because they make a real difference in the final output. Five standard paper sizes are available: A3 for large-format printing, A4 as the international standard, A5 for half-page booklet layouts, Letter for North American documents, and Legal for contracts and formal filings. The "Fit to Image" option takes a different approach entirely — it sizes each PDF page to match the exact dimensions of the corresponding image, which is useful when you're working with photographs or screenshots where you want to preserve the original proportions without any scaling or cropping. Orientation can be set to Portrait or Landscape, applied globally across all pages. If you're working with a mix of image types — some portrait-oriented photographs alongside some landscape screenshots — the Fit to Image option handles this gracefully by giving each page its own dimensions rather than forcing every image into the same-sized box. This can produce a PDF where pages vary in size, which is perfectly valid in the PDF specification and handled correctly by all modern readers, though it may look slightly unusual when scrolling through the document.
ipdf.s2.body2: Margins define the blank space between the image edges and the page borders. The No Margin option produces full-bleed layouts where the image extends to every edge — good for photography portfolios and visual presentations where you want maximum image area. Small (5mm), Medium (10mm), and Large (20mm) margins create progressively more framing around the image, which gives content room to breathe on the printed page and looks more professional for document-oriented PDFs. The Image Fit mode controls how each image is scaled relative to the page. "Contain" scales the image to fit entirely within the page while maintaining its original aspect ratio — this is the safest option because it guarantees no part of the image gets cut off. "Fill" scales the image to cover the entire page, which eliminates blank space but may crop the top, bottom, or sides of the image. "Original" places the image at its native resolution without any scaling, centered on the page. For documents that will be printed, margin choice affects both aesthetics and practicality. Printers typically can't render ink all the way to the edge of the paper, so a document with no margins may have its edges clipped when printed on a standard home or office printer. Adding at least a small margin ensures that the full image appears on the printed page. For on-screen viewing, margins matter less from a technical standpoint but still contribute to a polished, professional appearance that reflects well on the content.
ipdf.s3.label: Easy workflow
ipdf.s3.title: Drag-and-drop interface with real-time preview
ipdf.s3.body1: Image quality in the output PDF is controlled by a slider that ranges from 50 to 100 percent. This setting determines how much additional JPEG compression is applied to each image before it gets embedded in the PDF. At 100 percent, images are embedded with minimal compression, preserving maximum detail at the cost of larger file sizes. At 50 percent, compression is more aggressive, producing significantly smaller files but introducing subtle artifacts — slightly fuzzy edges, reduced color accuracy in gradients, and occasional blurring of fine details like thin text or narrow lines. For most purposes, a quality setting between 85 and 95 percent provides the best practical balance. The difference between 95 and 100 percent is often imperceptible visually but can add 20 to 40 percent to the file size, especially for images with lots of detail or variation. One practical tip: if your source images are already compressed JPEG files — which most phone photos are — setting the quality slider to 95 or 100 percent won't actually improve their appearance, because the original image data has already been lossy-compressed by your phone's camera. In that case, a setting around 85 to 90 percent produces visually identical output at a smaller file size. The quality slider matters most when your source images are uncompressed PNG or TIFF files, where the slider controls the first and only round of lossy compression applied to the image data.
ipdf.s3.body2: The preview panel on the right side of the settings area shows how each page of the PDF will look with your current settings applied. You can navigate between pages using the arrow buttons to check that every image is positioned correctly, that the margins are even, and that nothing unexpected is happening with the scaling. This preview runs entirely in your browser — it renders each page on a canvas element in real time as you change settings, so there's no delay or regeneration step. If an image looks wrong in the preview — maybe it's getting cropped when you wanted it to fit entirely, or the margins are too tight for a document that needs to look clean when printed — you can adjust the relevant setting and see the result immediately. This live feedback loop eliminates the common frustration of generating a PDF, opening it, discovering a problem, going back to adjust settings, and regenerating. This immediate visual feedback is one of the key advantages of client-side processing. Server-based tools typically require you to generate the full PDF and download it before you can see what it looks like, which makes the iterate-and-adjust cycle much slower. With this tool, adjusting a setting and seeing its effect is instantaneous, which encourages experimentation and leads to better results. You can try different margin sizes, switch between contain and fill modes, or compare portrait versus landscape orientation in seconds.
ipdf.s4.label: Privacy
ipdf.s4.title: Your files never leave your device
ipdf.s5.label: Use cases
ipdf.s5.title: Perfect for documents, presentations, and archives
ipdf.s5.body1: The decision to process everything locally in the browser wasn't made for technical convenience — it was made because document privacy is a genuine concern that most online PDF tools don't adequately address. When you upload a scanned contract to a conversion service, you're sending potentially sensitive legal terms to a server operated by a company you may know nothing about. When you upload medical records, tax documents, or financial statements, you're exposing personal data to whatever security practices that service happens to maintain. This tool sidesteps that entire risk category by keeping the processing on your device. The jsPDF library runs inside your browser's JavaScript sandbox, reads your image files from local memory, constructs the PDF document structure in memory, and creates a downloadable Blob object. At no point during this process does any data leave your machine. In an era of increasing data breach awareness, this approach offers genuine peace of mind. You don't need to read through a lengthy privacy policy to understand what happens to your documents, because the answer is straightforward: nothing happens to them outside your device. There's no server to breach, no database to compromise, no backup tape to lose, no employee with excessive access permissions. The attack surface for your document data is effectively zero because the data never enters any system that you don't personally control.
ipdf.s5.body2: This local-only architecture matters more in certain contexts than others. If you work in healthcare, you're dealing with patient data protected by regulations like HIPAA that impose strict requirements on how information is transmitted and stored. If you're in finance, client documents are subject to SEC and banking privacy rules. If you handle legal documents, attorney-client privilege creates obligations around data handling. Even outside of regulated industries, there's a basic expectation that business contracts, employee records, and internal correspondence should be treated with appropriate care. By eliminating server involvement entirely, this tool removes the need to evaluate a third-party service's security certifications, data retention policies, geographic server locations, and compliance claims — because none of those factors apply when the processing never leaves your browser.
ipdf.s6.label: About this tool

ipdf.s6.body1: The actual workflow breaks down into four steps that take most people under a minute from start to finish. The PDF doesn't impose any usage restrictions, passwords, or digital rights management controls. It's a straightforward document file that behaves exactly like any other PDF you'd create or receive.
ipdf.s6.body3: Common use cases span a wide range of professional and personal scenarios. Scanning and combining multi-page documents into a single navigable PDF is probably the most frequent one. pdf.extract.s1.body: The PDF Text Extractor is an online document text extraction tool that quickly extracts all text content from PDF documents containing a text layer, outputting editable, copyable, and saveable plain text. Many PDFs restrict text selection, or have complex layouts that cause garbled formatting, misaligned line breaks, and merged paragraphs when copied — manual handling is time-consuming and error-prone. This tool directly parses the document text layer and outputs the complete text in original reading order, eliminating the tedious task of copying paragraph by paragraph.
