pdf.merge.s1.label: PDF Merging
pdf.merge.s1.title: Combine Multiple PDFs into One Document
pdf.merge.s1.body: You just finished a client proposal that spans three separate PDFs — the cover letter in one file, the pricing breakdown in another, and the terms and conditions in a third. You need to send this as a single document, not as three attachments the client has to juggle. Or maybe you downloaded a white paper that was split into five chapter files, and you want to read it as one continuous document on your tablet during a flight. Or you are an accountant who just received 14 separate invoice PDFs from a vendor and you need to file them as a single document for the audit trail. Merging PDFs is one of those tasks that sounds trivial until you actually need to do it, at which point you discover that most solutions are surprisingly bad. Adobe Acrobat can do it, but you need a paid license and the interface for arranging merge order is not what you would call intuitive. Online merge tools are everywhere, but they all want you to upload your files to their servers, which is fine for a recipe PDF but a serious problem when the files contain salary data, patient information, or attorney-client communications. Our PDF Merge tool takes the straightforward approach: you upload files directly into your browser, drag them into the order you want, and click merge. The combined PDF is generated locally on your machine and downloads instantly. The 50 MB per-file limit covers the vast majority of business documents, and there is no cap on the number of files in a single merge. The operation is lossless because the tool works at the PDF structure level — it reads the page tree from each input document and constructs a new combined page tree that references the original content streams. Your text stays selectable, images keep their resolution, embedded fonts travel with their pages, and form fields keep working. The merged file is functionally identical to a document that was originally created as one file. It opens in every PDF reader, prints correctly, and can be searched, annotated, or signed with standard tools. The entire process runs in your browser with no server upload, which means it works the same way on your work laptop, your home computer, or a borrowed machine in a hotel business center.
pdf.merge.s2.label: Drag & Drop
pdf.merge.s2.title: Intuitive Drag-and-Drop Ordering
pdf.merge.s2.body1: The order of documents in the merged output is not something you want to guess at. If you are combining a cover letter, a proposal body, and a pricing appendix, those need to appear in exactly that sequence — not in whatever random order the browser happened to finish loading them. Our tool gives you a drag-and-drop interface where each uploaded file appears as a row with its filename, page count, and file size visible. You grab a row and drag it to where it belongs in the sequence, and the surrounding rows shift to accommodate. There is no ambiguity about what position 1 means versus position 3, because you arranged them yourself and the interface shows numbered position markers next to each file. This matters more than you might think. Command-line tools like pdftk or Ghostscript require you to specify merge order by listing file paths in sequence, which means you have to either rename your files alphabetically or memorize the exact order of a dozen file names. Some online merge tools combine files in the order they finish uploading — which is essentially random if your files vary in size, since a 2 MB document uploads faster than a 12 MB one. Our approach puts you in control of the sequence explicitly. Each file row has a remove button, so you can upload ten files, decide seven of them belong in the merge, and strip out the three outliers without starting the upload process from scratch. The drag system works with mouse input on desktop and touch input on tablets, which covers the devices where this kind of work typically happens.
pdf.merge.s2.body2: You can add more files at any point during the process. Say you have arranged six documents in the right order and then remember there is a seventh — an appendix that goes at the end. You do not need to start over. Click the add button, select the extra file, and drag it into position. The existing arrangement stays intact, and the new file slots in wherever you place it. There is no practical limit on the number of files in a single merge — the only constraint is that each individual file must be under 50 MB. We have tested merges with over 30 input files and the behavior is consistent. The tool displays a running total of combined pages and estimated output size as you add and rearrange files. If you are merging eight documents that total 340 pages and 18 MB, you see those numbers update in real time as you modify the list. This helps you catch mistakes early — if the page count seems too low, you probably forgot to include a file. When the arrangement looks right, one click on the merge button generates the combined PDF and starts the download. For a typical set of five to ten business documents, the entire process from first upload to finished download takes under a minute. The interface also displays individual file thumbnails where the browser can render them, which helps you visually confirm you have the right documents before committing to the merge. This is particularly useful when your files have generic names like document1.pdf and document2.pdf and you need to verify which is which.
pdf.merge.s3.label: Quality Preserved
pdf.merge.s3.title: Lossless PDF Merging
pdf.merge.s3.body: There is a technical distinction in how PDF merging can work that has a huge impact on your output. The quick-and-dirty approach, used by many online tools, is to render each page as a raster image — basically take a screenshot of every page — and then package those screenshots into a new PDF. This produces a file that looks right on screen but has been hollowed out internally. Text is now a picture of text: you cannot select it, you cannot search for a phrase across the document, you cannot copy a paragraph into an email. Screen readers cannot parse it, which creates accessibility problems. File sizes typically explode because each page is now a full-resolution JPEG or PNG — a 3 MB collection of text-heavy documents can balloon to 25 MB after being image-merged. Vector graphics like your company logo lose their resolution independence and look slightly fuzzy when you zoom in. Form fields stop accepting input, hyperlinks become inert text, and annotations vanish. Our tool uses pdf-lib.js, which works at the PDF object level instead. When you merge three PDFs, the library reads the page tree from each document, then constructs a new page tree that points to the original content streams from all three. The actual binary data for each page — the text operators, the font references, the image XObjects, the annotation arrays — is copied byte-for-byte into the merged output. Nothing is rendered, decoded, re-encoded, or substituted. The result is a merged document where text is fully selectable and searchable, vector graphics maintain their resolution independence at any zoom level, embedded fonts travel with the pages that reference them, hyperlinks work, form fields accept input, and bookmarks are preserved. The file size of the merged output is essentially the sum of your input files, plus a few kilobytes of overhead for the combined document catalog and cross-reference table. A 2 MB document merged with a 4 MB document produces a 6 MB output, not an 18 MB one.
pdf.merge.s4.label: Privacy
pdf.merge.s4.title: Files Never Leave Your Device
pdf.merge.s4.body1: Merging documents is a uniquely sensitive operation because the output file contains everything from every source document. If you are combining three PDFs — an NDA, a project scope document, and a vendor pricing sheet — the merged file contains confidential terms, project details, and pricing data all in one place. Now imagine uploading those three files to a merge service running on AWS. Your files hit an Application Load Balancer, get routed to an ECS container, the container writes them to its attached EBS volume for processing, the merged output gets written to an S3 bucket for the download link, and a CloudFront distribution serves it back to you. The service promises to delete everything after 24 hours, but that S3 bucket has versioning enabled, the EBS volume gets snapshotted nightly, and the application logs contain the file metadata including filenames that reveal your client's name. You have no way to verify any of this, and even if the service is perfectly responsible today, an acquisition or policy change tomorrow could alter the equation. Our tool avoids this entire category of risk by doing the merge in your browser. When you select files, the browser's File API reads each one into an in-memory ArrayBuffer. The pdf-lib.js library takes those ArrayBuffers, parses the PDF structure from each, and constructs the merged document entirely within the JavaScript runtime. The output is generated as a Blob in browser memory and delivered to you via URL.createObjectURL, which is a purely local operation. No HTTP request ever carries your file data. No WebSocket streams it externally. No ServiceWorker caches it for later.
pdf.merge.s4.body2: The pdf-lib.js library then processes these in-memory byte arrays to construct the merged document, all within the browser's JavaScript runtime. No network requests are made carrying your file data, no WebSocket connections stream content externally, and no service workers cache file bytes for later use. The merged output is generated as a Blob in browser memory and converted to a download trigger using the URL.createObjectURL API — a purely local operation. This architecture means your documents are never exposed to any third party at any point in the process. For professionals in regulated industries, this is a critical distinction. Merging legal contracts, financial statements, medical records, or HR documents using a cloud service could constitute a data disclosure event under regulations like GDPR, HIPAA, or SOX. With our browser-based tool, no data processing agreements are needed, no cross-border transfer assessments are required, and no breach notification procedures apply because no data ever leaves your control. The privacy guarantee is enforced by the browser's security model itself, not by a privacy policy that could change.
pdf.merge.s5.label: Use Cases
pdf.merge.s5.title: When You Need to Merge PDFs
pdf.merge.s5.body1: PDF merging is a daily necessity across dozens of professional contexts. In business administration, teams routinely merge monthly reports, meeting minutes, and project documentation into single files for executive review and archival. Sales departments combine proposals, pricing sheets, and terms into unified client packages that make a professional impression during negotiations. HR teams merge onboarding documents — offer letters, policy acknowledgments, benefits enrollment forms — into complete employee packets that new hires can review and sign as a single document. Finance departments consolidate invoices, purchase orders, and expense reports into single PDFs for accounts payable processing and audit trails, saving auditors from opening dozens of individual files. In education, teachers merge worksheets and handouts into weekly assignment packets, while university administrators combine transcripts, recommendation letters, and application forms for admission committee review. Students merge lecture notes, reading summaries, and assignment submissions into comprehensive study materials that are easier to manage than scattered individual files. Government agencies merge public records, correspondence, and supporting documents into unified disclosure packages for FOIA requests and public information releases.
pdf.merge.s5.body2: The legal industry is one of the heaviest users of PDF merging. Law firms routinely combine pleadings, exhibits, declarations, and supporting documents into court filing packages that must be submitted as a single PDF — most courts now require electronic filing with strict formatting rules about combining documents. Real estate professionals merge property disclosures, inspection reports, title documents, and closing statements into transaction files that buyers, sellers, and escrow officers can reference as a single authoritative document. Insurance adjusters combine claim forms, photographs, estimates, and correspondence into claim files that tell the complete story of a claim from first notice to final settlement. In creative and marketing fields, designers merge multi-page layouts exported from different design tools into a single publication, ensuring brand consistency across materials created by different team members. Agencies combine creative briefs, mood boards, and mockups into client presentation documents that provide a complete visual narrative. Publishers merge chapters from different authors into complete manuscripts before sending them for copy editing and typesetting. Healthcare administrators merge patient intake forms, insurance verification documents, and consent forms into unified patient records. Even for personal use, merging PDFs is invaluable — combining tax documents before filing, assembling travel reservations and confirmations into a single trip file, or merging scanned pages of personal records into organized digital archives. Whatever your profession or personal need, our tool makes the merge process fast, reliable, and completely private.
pdf.merge.s6.label: About This Tool
pdf.merge.s6.title: Free, Unlimited, and Instant
pdf.merge.s6.body1: This PDF Merge tool was designed from the start to be the simplest and most trustworthy way to combine PDF files. It is completely free with no watermarks added to the merged output, no limit on the number of files you can merge per operation, no daily usage caps, and no registration required. There are no premium tiers or feature gates — every capability of the tool is available to every user from the first visit. The tool works on all modern browsers across all major operating systems, including Chrome, Firefox, Safari, and Edge on Windows, macOS, Linux, and ChromeOS. The interface loads quickly and the merge operation begins processing as soon as you click the button, with no queues, no waiting for server capacity, and no advertisements interrupting your workflow. Performance scales naturally with your hardware — the tool uses your CPU and memory rather than competing for shared server resources, so merging a batch of large files on a capable machine is noticeably faster than struggling with a slow cloud service during peak hours.
pdf.merge.s6.body2: The underlying technology is pdf-lib.js, a mature and well-maintained open-source library for creating and modifying PDF files in JavaScript. It supports a wide range of PDF specifications and has been extensively tested against files generated by Microsoft Office, Google Docs, Adobe Acrobat, LibreOffice, Apple Pages, and numerous other applications. The library handles the complexities of PDF merging — reconciling duplicate font names, managing shared resource dictionaries, preserving cross-reference tables, and maintaining proper page label numbering — so you do not have to think about any of it. You simply upload, arrange, and merge. The output is a standards-compliant PDF that opens correctly in every PDF reader, prints faithfully, and can be further edited or signed with any standard PDF tool. The library also correctly handles edge cases like PDFs with different page sizes, mixed orientations, and varying PDF version numbers.
pdf.merge.s6.body3: We built this tool because we believe basic document operations like merging PDFs should be free, fast, and private. No one should have to install trial-ware, create an account, or upload sensitive files to an unknown server just to combine two PDFs. Whether you are a seasoned professional who merges documents daily or someone who needs to combine files for a one-time task, this tool is here to help. Open the page, upload your files, drag them into order, and download the result. It really is that simple. The tool is actively maintained and tested against new browser versions and PDF specifications as they are released, ensuring reliable performance over time. Try it now and see how effortless PDF merging can be when the tool is designed around your needs rather than around a subscription model.

pdf.split.s2.label: Core Features

pdf.split.s2.body1: Page Range mode: specify exact page intervals (e.g. 1-3, 5, 8-10) to extract target pages as a standalone PDF. Fixed Interval mode: automatically split every N pages — perfect for separating batch documents of uniform format (invoices, contracts, transcripts, shipping labels) without having to specify each one manually. Single Page mode: generates a separate PDF for each page, ideal for single-page distribution, page-by-page printing, or individual archiving, with automatic naming and numbering.
pdf.split.s2.body2: All three modes support custom output filename prefixes and ZIP packaging options. After splitting, download files individually or bundle them into a single ZIP archive, flexibly adapting to different file management needs.

pdf.split.s3.title: Lossless Splitting — Original Content Preserved Intact
pdf.split.s3.body: Splitting operates at the PDF structure level, extracting specified pages from the original document without any content re-encoding. Text, images, fonts, layouts, hyperlinks, bookmarks, and all other original attributes are fully preserved. Each split file is identical to the corresponding pages in the original document, with file sizes proportionally distributed by page count — no quality degradation or file size bloat. Suitable for splitting format-sensitive documents such as contracts, proposals, and financial statements. Even documents with complex tables, vector graphics, or high-resolution images maintain their precise layout after splitting, and can be directly used for formal submissions, legal archiving, or audit records.

Processing does not depend on network transmission and works even in offline environments.
pdf.split.s4.body2: When you close the page after splitting, file data is released from memory. No cache is left behind, nothing is stored on any backend, and no trace remains — ensuring data security. Even in shared office environments or on public computers, you can use this tool with confidence.

pdf.encrypt.s1.title: AES-256 Password Protection for PDF Documents
pdf.encrypt.s1.body: The PDF Encrypt tool is an online document security solution that applies AES-256 bit encryption to password-protect PDF documents. It supports a dual-password mechanism: the user password controls document opening permissions, while the owner password finely controls six operation permissions—printing, copying, modifying, annotating, filling forms, and page assembly. Whether protecting business contracts, encrypting financial reports, restricting confidential file distribution, or setting access thresholds for externally shared documents, this tool delivers professional-grade security protection. The encryption process is completed locally in the browser, with files remaining entirely on the user's device throughout—no network transmission involved. Clear operation flow: import file, set passwords and permissions, execute encryption, download result—four steps to completion, ready to use from the first attempt.

Even highly confidential files containing sensitive information receive bank-level data protection.
pdf.encrypt.s2.body2: The encryption process is completed locally in the browser, with files remaining entirely on the user's device throughout—no network transmission involved. pdf.encrypt.s3.title: Dual-Password Mechanism with Six Granular Permission Controls
pdf.encrypt.s3.body: Supports user password and owner password dual protection mechanism. The user password is what recipients need to enter when opening the document; the owner password controls recipient operation permissions on the document. Provides six refined permission settings: print permission—controls whether document content printing is allowed; copy permission—controls whether text and image copying is allowed; modify permission—controls whether document content editing is allowed; annotate permission—controls whether adding comments and annotations is allowed; form fill permission—controls whether filling interactive forms is allowed; page assembly permission—controls whether inserting, deleting, or rotating pages is allowed. Each permission can be toggled independently, flexibly adapting to different sharing scenarios' security requirements—from fully locked to partially open—achieving precise access control strategies.

pdf.compress.s1.label: Product Overview
pdf.compress.s1.title: Smart PDF Compression — Smaller Files, Uncompromised Quality
pdf.compress.s1.body: This PDF compression tool is a professional-grade document optimizer that reduces file size by re-encoding embedded images and stripping redundant metadata, while preserving visual quality and layout integrity. Large PDF files create friction across countless workflows — email attachments exceeding size limits, sluggish website downloads, slow mobile rendering, and perpetually strained storage. This tool resolves all of these issues in a single click. Three compression tiers are available: Screen mode (72 DPI), optimized for web sharing and email attachments to produce the smallest possible file; eBook mode (150 DPI), striking an optimal balance between size and clarity for digital reading and everyday office use; and Printer mode (300 DPI), maintaining high-fidelity output for professional printing and formal documentation. Upon completion, the compression ratio and space saved are displayed in real time, providing immediate visibility into the optimization results. Additional options include stripping document metadata and flattening form fields to further reduce file size. The entire compression process runs locally in the browser, keeping files on the user's device at all times. The workflow is straightforward: import a file, select a compression level, execute, and download — four steps that anyone can follow from the very first use.
pdf.compress.s2.label: Core Features
pdf.compress.s2.title: Three Compression Tiers, Precisely Tailored to Every Use Case
pdf.compress.s2.body1: Screen mode (72 DPI) delivers the highest compression ratio, generating the smallest possible file size. It is ideal for scenarios where file size is paramount — web publishing, social media sharing, and email attachments. Image assets are re-encoded to screen resolution, stripping print-redundant data, which can reduce file size to 10%–30% of the original. This dramatically improves transfer speed and storage efficiency.
pdf.compress.s2.body2: eBook mode (150 DPI) achieves the optimal balance between file size and visual quality, making it well-suited for digital reading, tablet browsing, and everyday document sharing. Printer mode (300 DPI) maintains high-fidelity output, applying moderate compression solely through image encoding optimization and metadata removal, ensuring print quality remains unaffected for formal documents that require hardcopy output. All three modes preserve the document's complete layout, text clarity, and interactive functionality.
pdf.compress.s3.label: Output Quality
pdf.compress.s3.title: Intelligent Image Re-Encoding and Metadata Refinement
pdf.compress.s3.body: The compression engine intelligently identifies the most space-consuming elements in a PDF — embedded images — and re-encodes them at the resolution and quality parameters corresponding to the selected tier. Text content and vector graphics retain their original precision and remain entirely unaffected; only bitmap assets receive targeted optimization, ensuring the compressed document is virtually indistinguishable from the original in visual appearance. The tool also supports stripping document metadata (author information, creation dates, modification history, and more), which simultaneously reduces file size and removes potentially sensitive personal information. The form field flattening feature converts interactive forms into static content, further streamlining the file structure. These three optimization techniques, used individually or in combination, maximize compression results without compromising the reading experience.

ocr.s1.body1: Think about the last time you needed to grab text from an image. Maybe it was a quote in a book you wanted to save, a phone number on a flyer, or an entire page of notes you'd handwritten during a meeting. Before OCR technology became widely accessible, your only real option was to type it all out manually — a slow, error-prone process that nobody enjoys. Optical Character Recognition changes that equation entirely. At its core, OCR analyzes the visual patterns in an image and maps them to characters in a digital alphabet. Modern engines have gotten remarkably good at this, thanks to deep learning models trained on enormous datasets of labeled text images. The result is a technology that can handle messy handwriting, unusual fonts, rotated text, and even documents where multiple languages appear side by side. This particular tool wraps that capability into a straightforward browser-based interface: you upload or paste an image, the engine processes it, and you get editable text back in a matter of seconds. There's no software to install, no sign-up form to fill out, and no learning curve to speak of. It works the way you'd expect it to — drag, drop, read, edit, and you're done.
ocr.s1.body2: What makes OCR genuinely useful — as opposed to just technically impressive — is how it bridges the gap between the physical and digital worlds without requiring any specialized equipment beyond the smartphone camera you already carry in your pocket. Before this technology was readily available, getting text off a printed page meant either typing it out by hand or investing in a dedicated scanner with bundled OCR software. Both options were slow, expensive, or both. Today, a quick photo is enough. The recognition engines that power modern OCR have been trained on billions of text samples, and they've gotten frighteningly good at handling the kind of messy, real-world images that people actually capture — photos taken at angles, under mixed lighting, with shadows falling across the page, or containing text in multiple sizes and styles mixed together with graphics and background elements.
ocr.s3.body1: Let's talk about what happens to your image during this process, because it matters more than most people realize. The image travels directly from your browser to the OCR engine over an encrypted connection. It is not stored on any intermediate server, and the OCR provider deletes it immediately after processing completes — not after an hour, not after a day, but immediately. This is a deliberate architectural choice, and it's worth understanding why. If you're running a business and processing invoices, you're handling financial data. If you're a lawyer digitizing contracts, you're dealing with attorney-client privilege. If you're a healthcare worker scanning patient forms, you're in HIPAA territory. In all of these cases, the last thing you want is for a copy of that document to linger on someone else's server in a data center you've never heard of. This tool was built with that concern front and center. No accounts, no tracking cookies, no analytics that could potentially be linked back to the documents you've processed. It's a one-shot transaction: your image goes in, text comes out, and nothing is retained.
ocr.s3.body2: This approach stands in sharp contrast to the data practices of many free online tools, which often fund their operations by collecting user data, displaying targeted advertisements, or building analytics profiles from the documents people process. The absence of any account system here is not an oversight — it's a deliberate design choice that means there's no way to associate processed documents with individual users, because the system never asks for or stores any identifying information. For organizations that need to demonstrate compliance with data protection regulations, this architecture significantly simplifies the compliance picture: there's simply no data to protect, retain, or disclose because nothing is ever collected in the first place.
itv.s1.body1: There's a specific kind of frustration that comes from having a folder full of great photos and no easy way to present them as a video. You know the feeling: you've got twenty or thirty images from a trip, an event, or a product shoot, and you need to turn them into something that plays smoothly — not a jittery slideshow that jumps between frames, but an actual video with transitions, timing that feels natural, and output quality that looks good when shared. Most of the solutions out there fall into one of two camps. Cloud-based tools want you to upload everything to their servers, wait in a processing queue, and then download the result — assuming their free tier doesn't cut you off halfway through. Desktop software gives you more control but requires installation, often costs money, and tends to be overkill if you just need a straightforward image sequence converted to video. This tool takes a different approach. It uses two browser-native web APIs — Canvas for rendering frames and MediaRecorder for encoding video — to handle the entire process locally on your device. Your images stay on your computer. The rendering starts immediately. And the output quality rivals what you'd get from dedicated software for the vast majority of slideshow and image-sequence use cases.
itv.s1.body2: The practical upshot of this browser-native approach is that you get desktop-quality video generation without any of the friction that usually comes with it. No download and install cycle, no license activation, no system compatibility checks, no updates to manage. Open the page in any modern browser — Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge — and you have everything you need. The tool works the same way on Windows, macOS, Linux, ChromeOS, and even tablets with capable browsers. This cross-platform consistency is one of the underrated advantages of browser-based tools: you get the same experience and the same output quality regardless of what device you're using, which is particularly valuable in collaborative environments where team members use different operating systems.
itv.s3.body1: Resolution and frame rate are the two technical parameters that most directly affect how your output video looks. This tool offers three resolution presets. The 480p option produces a video at 854 by 480 pixels — fine for messaging apps and situations where file size matters more than visual fidelity. The 720p HD option at 1280 by 720 is the sweet spot for most purposes: it looks sharp on phones and tablets, loads quickly on social media, and keeps file sizes reasonable. The 1080p Full HD option at 1920 by 1080 gives you maximum detail, which matters if the video will be displayed on a large screen or if the images contain text or fine detail that needs to remain legible. Frame rate is a separate but related choice. 24fps gives the traditional cinematic look with subtle motion blur between frames. 30fps produces smooth, natural-feeling motion that works for almost everything. 60fps is noticeably more fluid, which you'll see most clearly during transitions. 15fps is available if you need a slightly stylized look or want to minimize file size. The tool outputs in WebM format by default (using the VP9 codec), which produces smaller files and plays in all modern browsers. MP4 is also available for compatibility with older software and certain social media platforms that don't accept WebM.
itv.s3.body2: For output format, the tool defaults to WebM, which uses Google's VP9 codec to produce excellent quality at smaller file sizes than equivalent H.264 MP4 files. WebM plays natively in Chrome, Firefox, Edge, and Opera, which covers the vast majority of desktop and Android browser usage. Safari has also added WebM support in recent versions. If you need maximum compatibility — for uploading to a platform that requires MP4, for editing in software that doesn't support WebM, or for sharing with someone using an older device — the MP4 option is available as well. The quality difference between the two formats at the same resolution and frame rate is negligible for typical slideshow content, so the choice mostly comes down to where the video will be used and who needs to play it.
ipdf.s1.body1: At some point, almost everyone runs into the same problem: you've got a bunch of images that really should be a single document. Maybe they're scanned pages of a contract, photos of receipts you need to submit for an expense report, screenshots of an email thread you want to archive, or product photos you'd like to send as one file instead of twenty separate attachments. Converting images to PDF is the natural solution, but the tools available for doing this tend to be either cumbersome or questionable from a privacy standpoint. Upload-based services ask you to send your images to a server you know nothing about, which is fine for a meme but problematic when the images contain financial information, legal documents, or anything else you'd rather keep private. Desktop software works but requires installation and often includes features you don't need and a price tag you'd rather avoid. This tool takes a third path: it generates the PDF entirely within your browser using jsPDF, a JavaScript library that builds valid PDF documents without any server involvement. Your images stay on your device from start to finish. The resulting PDF is a standard document that opens in any PDF reader on any platform.
ipdf.s1.body2: The jsPDF library that powers this tool has been around for over a decade and is one of the most widely used JavaScript PDF generation libraries in the world. It produces fully valid PDF documents that comply with the ISO 32000 standard, which means the output files will open correctly in every PDF reader application currently in existence — from Adobe Acrobat to Apple Preview to the built-in viewers in web browsers to mobile PDF apps on every platform. The library handles all of the low-level PDF structure work internally: page tree management, cross-reference tables, font embedding, image stream compression, and metadata generation. You don't need to think about any of this. You just configure your settings, click the button, and get a standard PDF file.
ipdf.s4.body1: Input format support covers the image types you'd expect: JPG, PNG, WebP, GIF, BMP, and TIFF. Up to 50 images can be combined into a single PDF. The upload interface supports drag-and-drop from your desktop, standard file picker selection, and multi-file selection for adding several images at once. Once uploaded, images appear as numbered thumbnails in a grid. Drag any thumbnail to a new position to reorder the sequence — the numbers update automatically, and the page order in the final PDF will match the grid order exactly. The Add More button lets you insert additional images at any point without starting over. Individual images can be removed by hovering over them and clicking the remove button. The Clear All button resets everything. Each grid thumbnail shows a small preview of the image and its position number, so you can verify the sequence at a glance before generating the PDF. The file picker supports multi-selection using the standard Ctrl+click (or Command+click on Mac) modifier, so you can add twenty images in one operation rather than uploading them individually. The drag-and-drop interface also accepts files dropped from any file manager or desktop folder, and you can drop multiple files at once. These input methods might seem like small details, but they make a real difference in usability when you're working with large batches of images — the difference between a workflow that takes thirty seconds and one that takes five minutes of repetitive clicking.
ipdf.s4.body2: One detail worth noting: the order of images in the grid determines the page order in the final PDF, and this order is preserved exactly — no automatic sorting, no alphabetical reordering, no unexpected rearrangement. If you drag an image to position three, it will appear on page three of the PDF. This explicit control over page order is particularly important when combining scanned document pages, where the correct sequence is essential for readability and legal validity. The drag-and-drop reordering is responsive and works well on both desktop and tablet screens. On mobile devices, the grid switches to a compact two-column layout that still allows reordering, though the experience is more comfortable on a larger screen. This attention to order preservation might sound obvious, but it's actually a common source of frustration with other tools that silently re-sort files alphabetically or by creation date, which can scramble the intended page sequence of multi-page scanned documents.

img.compress.guide.hero.title: Image Compression Guide
img.compress.guide.hero.subtitle: Everything you need to know about compressing JPG, PNG, and WebP images for the web — from quality settings to batch workflows.
img.compress.guide.toc.title: Table of Contents
img.compress.guide.s1.title: What is image compression?
img.compress.guide.s1.p1: Image compression is the process of reducing the file size of an image while maintaining acceptable visual quality. Smaller files load faster, use less bandwidth, and take up less storage space. This is essential for websites, mobile apps, email attachments, and any scenario where image file size matters.
img.compress.guide.s1.p2: There are two main types of compression: lossy and lossless. Lossy compression discards some image data to achieve smaller files, while lossless compression preserves every pixel. The best approach depends on your use case and the image format you are working with.
img.compress.guide.s2.title: Lossy vs lossless compression
img.compress.guide.s2.p1: Lossy compression formats like JPEG and WebP achieve dramatic size reductions by removing data that the human eye is less likely to notice. This makes them ideal for photographs and complex images where some quality loss is acceptable in exchange for much smaller files.
img.compress.guide.s2.p2: Lossless formats like PNG preserve every pixel exactly, making them perfect for graphics, logos, screenshots, and any image where sharp edges or transparency are important. The trade-off is larger file sizes compared to lossy alternatives.
img.compress.guide.s3.title: Choosing the right format
img.compress.guide.s3.p1: JPEG is the universal standard for photographs. It offers excellent compression and is supported by virtually every device and browser. Use JPEG when you need broad compatibility and are working with photo-realistic images.
img.compress.guide.s3.p2: PNG is the go-to format for images that require transparency or pixel-perfect accuracy. Logos, icons, diagrams, and screenshots should generally be saved as PNG. WebP combines the best of both worlds — it supports transparency like PNG and offers compression efficiency like JPEG, making it the modern standard for web images.
img.compress.guide.s3.p3: WebP is a modern format that supports both lossy and lossless compression, plus transparency. It typically produces files 25%-35% smaller than JPG at the same visual quality, making it excellent for websites.
img.compress.guide.s4.title: Understanding quality settings
img.compress.guide.s4.p1: The quality slider controls the trade-off between file size and visual fidelity. At 90-100%, the compressed image is nearly indistinguishable from the original, making this range ideal for portfolios, product photography, and print preparation.
img.compress.guide.s4.p2: At 60-80%, most photographs still look excellent while achieving significant file size reductions. This is the sweet spot for blog images, social media posts, and general web use. At 30-50%, you get the smallest files with noticeable but often acceptable quality loss — useful for thumbnails, previews, and bandwidth-constrained environments.
img.compress.guide.s5.title: How browser-side compression works
img.compress.guide.s5.p1: Our tool uses the HTML5 Canvas API and WebAssembly to compress images entirely within your browser. When you drop an image onto the tool, it is decoded into pixel data, processed according to your settings, and re-encoded into the target format — all on your device.
img.compress.guide.s5.p2: This browser-side approach means your images never travel over the network. There is no upload queue, no server processing time, and no risk of your files being stored on a remote server. The entire workflow typically completes in seconds, even for batches of 20 images.
img.compress.guide.s6.title: Batch compression workflow
img.compress.guide.s6.p1: Batch compression lets you process multiple images with the same settings in one operation. Simply drag and drop up to 20 images, adjust your quality and format preferences, and the tool processes them all in parallel using your browser's multi-core capabilities.
img.compress.guide.s6.p2: Each image displays its own compression statistics, showing the original size, compressed size, and percentage saved. When processing is complete, you can download individual images or grab them all as a single ZIP file with one click.
img.compress.guide.s7.title: Setting maximum width and resizing
img.compress.guide.s7.p1: The maximum width setting automatically downscales oversized images while preserving their aspect ratio. This is especially useful when you have high-resolution photos from a camera or phone that are far larger than needed for web display.
img.compress.guide.s7.p2: By combining compression with resizing, you can achieve dramatic reductions in file size. A 4000-pixel-wide photograph from a modern smartphone might be 5MB or more. Resizing it to 1920 pixels wide and compressing at 80% quality can easily bring it under 300KB — a 94% reduction with no visible quality loss on screen.
img.compress.guide.s8.title: Compressing images for websites
img.compress.guide.s8.p1: Website performance is heavily influenced by image file sizes. Google's Core Web Vitals metrics, particularly Largest Contentful Paint (LCP), directly measure how quickly your main content loads. Large, unoptimized images are one of the most common causes of poor LCP scores.
img.compress.guide.s8.p2: For website hero images, use WebP at 80-85% quality with a maximum width of 1920px. For inline content images, 1200px width at 75% quality is usually sufficient. Always provide width and height attributes in your HTML to prevent layout shifts.
img.compress.guide.s9.title: Compressing images for email
img.compress.guide.s9.p1: Email clients have strict size limits for attachments and inline images. Most providers cap attachments at 10-25MB total, and many recipients view emails on mobile devices with limited data plans. Keeping individual images under 200KB ensures fast loading and reliable delivery.
img.compress.guide.s9.p2: For email newsletters, compress images to 600-800px width at 70-80% quality. Use JPEG for photos and PNG only when transparency is essential. Consider using a maximum width setting to ensure all images in your campaign have consistent dimensions.
img.compress.guide.s10.title: Compressing images for social media
img.compress.guide.s10.p1: Each social platform applies its own compression when you upload images. By compressing strategically before upload, you can minimize the additional quality loss imposed by the platform's algorithms. Instagram, Facebook, and Twitter all use aggressive compression on large files.
img.compress.guide.s10.p2: For Instagram posts, compress to 1080x1080px at 85% quality. For Twitter and Facebook, 1200x675px at 80% quality works well. LinkedIn prefers 1200x627px. Using our Image Resizer tool, you can batch-generate all platform-specific sizes from a single master image.
img.compress.guide.s11.title: Common compression mistakes
img.compress.guide.s11.p1: One of the most common mistakes is compressing an already-compressed image repeatedly. Each pass of lossy compression discards more data, leading to visible artifacts and banding. Always start from the highest-quality original you have available.
img.compress.guide.s11.p2: Another frequent error is using PNG for photographs. While PNG is lossless, a typical photo saved as PNG will be 5-10 times larger than the same image as a well-compressed JPEG. Reserve PNG for graphics, logos, and images that need transparency. Finally, avoid scaling images with CSS after uploading oversized files — compress and resize to the exact display dimensions instead.
img.compress.guide.s12.title: Advanced tips and best practices
img.compress.guide.s12.p1: For maximum efficiency, combine multiple optimizations: start with the right format, set an appropriate maximum width, choose a quality level suited to your use case, and batch process everything at once. Our preset buttons let you apply common configurations instantly — High Quality for portfolios, Balanced for general web use, Small File for thumbnails, and Tiny for previews.
img.compress.guide.s12.p2: Experiment with different settings to find the optimal balance for your specific images. Because our tool processes everything locally, you can try multiple configurations in seconds without waiting for server responses or consuming upload bandwidth. The real-time statistics show exactly how much you are saving, making it easy to compare approaches and choose the best one.

img.resize.guide.seo.title: Image Resize Guide — Dimensions, Formats & Best Practices | ToolboxNova
img.resize.guide.seo.desc: Complete guide to image resizing: understand dimensions, aspect ratios, Fit/Fill/Stretch modes, social media sizes, and web performance best practices.
img.resize.guide.hero.title: The Complete Image Resize Guide
img.resize.guide.hero.subtitle: Master image dimensions, aspect ratios, resize modes, and platform-specific sizes for web, social media, and e-commerce.
img.resize.guide.toc.title: Table of Contents
img.resize.guide.s1.title: What Is Image Resizing?
img.resize.guide.s1.p1: Image resizing is the process of changing the width and height of a digital image. Unlike compression which reduces file size while keeping dimensions the same, resizing actually changes the pixel count of the image — making it larger or smaller in terms of display size.
img.resize.guide.s1.p2: Resizing is essential for web performance, social media optimization, and cross-platform consistency. A 4000×3000 pixel photo from your camera might look great, but it's far too large for a website thumbnail or Instagram post. Proper resizing ensures your images look sharp while loading fast.
img.resize.guide.s2.title: Understanding Image Dimensions
img.resize.guide.s2.p1: Image dimensions are expressed as width × height in pixels. For example, 1920×1080 means the image is 1920 pixels wide and 1080 pixels tall. The total pixel count (width × height) directly affects both visual quality and file size.
img.resize.guide.s2.p2: When resizing, you can specify exact pixel values, scale by percentage, or use preset dimensions optimized for specific platforms. Larger dimensions mean more detail but bigger files. Finding the right balance is key to professional results.
img.resize.guide.s3.title: Resize Modes: Fit, Fill & Stretch
img.resize.guide.s3.p1: Our resizer offers three modes for different needs. Stretch forces the image to exact target dimensions, which may distort the aspect ratio. Fit maintains the original aspect ratio and scales the image to fit entirely within the target bounds — the output may be smaller than specified. Fill also maintains aspect ratio but scales to completely cover the target area, cropping any excess.
img.resize.guide.s3.p2: For most use cases, Fit is the safest choice when you want the entire image visible. Fill is ideal for hero banners and profile covers where the container must be completely covered. Stretch should only be used when exact dimensions are required and distortion is acceptable.
img.resize.guide.s3.box: Use Fill mode with Center crop focus for profile pictures and cover images. Use Fit mode when you need the entire image visible, such as product photos or illustrations.
img.resize.guide.s4.title: Social Media Image Size Reference
img.resize.guide.s4.p1: Each social platform has specific image size requirements. Instagram Post: 1080×1080px (square), Instagram Story: 1080×1920px (9:16). Facebook Post: 1200×630px, Facebook Cover: 820×312px. Twitter/X Post: 1200×675px, Twitter Header: 1500×500px. YouTube Thumbnail: 1280×720px, YouTube Banner: 2560×1440px.
img.resize.guide.s4.p2: LinkedIn Post: 1200×627px. Pinterest Pin: 1000×1500px (2:3 ratio performs best). TikTok Video: 1080×1920px. Using the exact recommended sizes ensures your images display at maximum quality without automatic cropping or compression by the platform.
img.resize.guide.s5.title: Website Image Sizes
img.resize.guide.s5.p1: For websites, the optimal size depends on context. Full-width hero images: 1920×1080px or 1920×600px. Content images: 1200×800px max. Blog featured images: 1200×630px (matches OG image size). Thumbnails: 300×300px or 150×150px. Icons: 64×64px or 32×32px.
img.resize.guide.s5.p2: Always consider the actual display size. If an image will be shown at 800px wide on screen, uploading a 4000px version wastes bandwidth and slows page load. Resize to 2× the display size for retina screens, then compress for optimal performance.
img.resize.guide.s6.title: E-commerce Product Image Sizes
img.resize.guide.s6.p1: E-commerce platforms have strict requirements. Amazon: 1000×1000px minimum (2000×2000px recommended), pure white background. Etsy: 2000×2000px recommended. eBay: 500×500px minimum. Shopify: 2048×2048px recommended for zoom functionality.
img.resize.guide.s6.p2: Product thumbnails are typically 300×300px. Category banners: 1200×400px. Consistent sizing across your store creates a professional appearance and improves user trust. Always check each platform's latest guidelines as they update periodically.
img.resize.guide.s7.title: Understanding Aspect Ratio
img.resize.guide.s7.p1: Aspect ratio is the proportional relationship between width and height, expressed as width:height. Common ratios include 1:1 (square), 4:3 (standard photo), 16:9 (widescreen), 3:2 (DSLR photo), and 9:16 (vertical video). Maintaining the original aspect ratio prevents distortion.
img.resize.guide.s7.p2: When you lock the aspect ratio in our resizer, changing one dimension automatically updates the other to maintain proportions. This is crucial for professional results. Common ratios for social: 1:1 for Instagram posts, 4:5 for Instagram portraits, 16:9 for Twitter/LinkedIn, 9:16 for Stories/Reels.
img.resize.guide.s7.box: Enable aspect ratio lock whenever possible. Only unlock it when you specifically need non-proportional dimensions, such as creating abstract backgrounds or fitting images into fixed-size containers.
img.resize.guide.s8.title: Choosing the Right Image Format
img.resize.guide.s8.p1: JPEG is best for photographs and complex images with many colors. It uses lossy compression and supports quality adjustment. PNG is ideal for graphics, logos, and images requiring transparency — it uses lossless compression but produces larger files.
img.resize.guide.s8.p2: WebP offers the best of both worlds: superior compression with transparency support. It's supported by all modern browsers and should be your default choice for web images. Our resizer supports conversion between all three formats during resizing.
img.resize.guide.s9.title: Performance Best Practices
img.resize.guide.s9.p1: Always resize images to their display dimensions before uploading. A common mistake is uploading camera originals (4000+ pixels wide) and letting the browser scale them down. This wastes bandwidth and slows page load significantly.
img.resize.guide.s9.p2: For responsive images, provide multiple sizes using the srcset attribute. Use WebP format when possible. After resizing, compress images to reduce file size further. Combined, these practices can reduce image payload by 70-90% without visible quality loss.
img.resize.guide.s10.title: Common Resizing Mistakes
img.resize.guide.s10.p1: Upsizing small images: Enlarging a 500px image to 2000px results in pixelation and blur. Always start with the highest resolution source. Ignoring aspect ratio: Stretching images to fit containers creates unprofessional distortion. Wrong format choice: Using PNG for photos creates unnecessarily large files.
img.resize.guide.s10.p2: Other mistakes include: not optimizing for retina displays (use 2× size), forgetting to compress after resizing, using inconsistent sizes across a website, and ignoring platform-specific requirements for social media. Our tool helps you avoid all of these with built-in presets and format conversion.

img.metadata.guide.s1.title: What is Image Metadata
img.metadata.guide.s1.p1: Image metadata is additional information embedded within image files that records various data about the image itself, but does not include the actual pixel content. Metadata can include image dimensions, format, color space, capture device, shooting parameters, capture time, GPS location, editing software, copyright info and more.
img.metadata.guide.s1.p2: Different image formats support different types of metadata. JPG most commonly contains EXIF data; PNG may include text chunks, XMP and ICC profiles; WebP also supports EXIF and XMP; TIFF and HEIC usually contain rich metadata.
img.metadata.guide.s2.title: What is EXIF
img.metadata.guide.s2.p1: EXIF (Exchangeable Image File Format) is the most common photo metadata standard, usually written automatically by digital cameras or smartphones when shooting. EXIF records shooting parameters (shutter speed, aperture, ISO, focal length), device info (camera brand, model), capture time, GPS coordinates and more.
img.metadata.guide.s2.p2: EXIF data is extremely valuable to photographers for analyzing shooting habits and reviewing parameters. But for privacy-conscious users, GPS and device info in EXIF may leak personal privacy when sharing images.
img.metadata.guide.s3.title: Privacy Information in Images
img.metadata.guide.s3.p1: Image metadata may contain various privacy-sensitive information: GPS coordinates can pinpoint the shooting location; capture time can reveal user habits; device model can be linked to specific users; editing software info can reveal workflow preferences; IPTC author and copyright info can directly expose identity.
img.metadata.guide.s3.box: Before publishing to social media or public platforms, check whether images contain sensitive information like GPS coordinates and capture time. Different platforms handle metadata differently - do not rely solely on automatic removal.
img.metadata.guide.s3.p2: Even after screenshots or compression, some metadata may remain. The safest approach is to use a dedicated metadata cleaning tool to check and remove sensitive info before publishing.
img.metadata.guide.s4.title: How to Check Capture Time & Device Info
img.metadata.guide.s4.p1: Using the ToolboxNova Image Metadata Viewer, simply upload a photo to see capture time, camera brand, model, lens, aperture, shutter speed, ISO and more in the Shooting Parameters panel. All parsing is done locally in your browser - images are never uploaded to any server.
img.metadata.guide.s4.p2: If the photo is a camera original, it typically contains complete EXIF info. But if the photo has been processed by social platforms, screenshotted, or saved via editing software's Export for Web, some or all EXIF info may have been removed.
img.metadata.guide.s5.title: How to Check if Images Contain GPS Location
img.metadata.guide.s5.p1: In our metadata viewer, if an image contains GPS info, it will display latitude, longitude and altitude in the GPS Location panel, with the shooting location marked on an OpenStreetMap.
img.metadata.guide.s5.p2: Not all photos contain GPS info. GPS coordinates are only embedded when the device had location services enabled during capture and the camera app had permission to write location data. Professional cameras typically do not record GPS unless an external GPS module is attached.
img.metadata.guide.s6.title: How to Remove EXIF Data from Images
img.metadata.guide.s6.p1: There are multiple ways to remove EXIF metadata from images: use an online metadata cleaning tool (like ToolboxNova), use Photoshop's Export with metadata disabled, use macOS Preview's Export function, or use command-line tools like ExifTool.
img.metadata.guide.s6.box: Screenshots are the simplest way to remove metadata, but they reduce image quality. A better approach is to use a dedicated metadata cleaning tool that selectively removes sensitive fields while preserving original image quality.
img.metadata.guide.s6.p2: When removing metadata, note that removing Orientation info may cause images to display in the wrong direction; removing ICC profiles may cause color discrepancies across different devices.
img.metadata.guide.s7.title: EXIF vs IPTC vs XMP vs ICC
img.metadata.guide.s7.p1: EXIF mainly records shooting parameters and device info, written automatically by cameras; IPTC is an international press telecommunications council standard mainly for copyright, author, title, keywords and other editorial info; XMP is Adobe's extensible metadata platform supporting more flexible structured data; ICC is the color profile ensuring consistent color display across devices.
img.metadata.guide.s7.p2: A professional photo may simultaneously contain all four metadata types: EXIF for shooting parameters, IPTC for copyright and description, XMP for Lightroom star ratings and labels, ICC for accurate color reproduction. Different software supports these metadata types to varying degrees.
img.metadata.guide.s8.title: Why Some Images Have No EXIF
img.metadata.guide.s8.p1: Common reasons for missing EXIF info include: screenshot software typically does not add EXIF; social platforms (like WeChat, Weibo) automatically compress and remove some metadata when uploading; some editing software exports without metadata by default; images saved directly from web pages usually contain no EXIF.
img.metadata.guide.s8.p2: Additionally, older cameras or specific use cases (scanned documents, composite images, AI-generated images) may not write EXIF data at all. Missing EXIF does not mean there is a problem with the image - it simply lacks shooting-related technical information.
img.metadata.guide.s9.title: Do Social Platforms Remove Image Metadata
img.metadata.guide.s9.p1: Most mainstream social platforms process or remove some metadata when users upload images, but the handling differs by platform. Instagram and Facebook typically remove GPS info; Twitter/X preserves some EXIF but removes GPS; WeChat usually compresses and removes metadata when forwarding images.
img.metadata.guide.s9.p2: Platform handling rules may change at any time, and behavior may also differ across clients (iOS/Android/Web). Therefore, before publishing sensitive images, you should not rely entirely on platforms to automatically remove metadata. Check and clean locally first.
img.metadata.guide.s10.title: Can Image Metadata Be Modified
img.metadata.guide.s10.p1: Yes, image metadata can be modified, added or deleted. Professional image editing software (like Photoshop, Lightroom) and command-line tools (like ExifTool) can all edit metadata. This means metadata cannot serve as absolute proof of image authenticity.
img.metadata.guide.s10.p2: Metadata can be forged: someone could add professional camera EXIF to their own images, or remove GPS info from original photos. Therefore, metadata should only be used as one reference point, not as standalone proof of image origin, authenticity or capture time.
img.metadata.guide.s11.title: Common Use Cases for Image Metadata
img.metadata.guide.s11.p1: Photographers use EXIF to review shooting parameters and analyze the effects of different settings; stock photo agencies use IPTC to track image ownership and licensing; privacy-conscious users check and remove GPS info before sharing; web admins use metadata for batch image library organization; developers use EXIF for automated image classification and processing.
img.metadata.guide.s11.p2: Digital forensics experts may use metadata as supporting evidence in investigations, but it must be combined with other evidence for comprehensive judgment. The most common need for everyday users is to quickly check whether personal photos contain location or device information they don't want to make public before posting online.
img.metadata.guide.s12.title: Frequently Asked Questions
img.metadata.guide.s12.p1: Viewing metadata does not upload images to any server. ToolboxNova's metadata viewer runs entirely in your browser using the exifr.js library to parse image files directly - no data is ever sent to any server.
img.metadata.guide.s12.p2: Metadata viewing supports JPG, PNG, WebP, TIFF and HEIC formats. Different formats support different metadata types: JPG usually contains the richest EXIF info; PNG and WebP may contain XMP, ICC and other text chunks; HEIC typically contains complete EXIF and XMP data.
img.metadata.guide.cta.title: Start Viewing Image Metadata
img.metadata.guide.cta.subtitle: Use our free browser-based metadata viewer - no upload, no registration, instantly view EXIF, GPS and privacy info.

guide.watermark.s1.body1: An image watermark is a visible overlay placed on top of a photo or graphic to identify ownership, protect copyright, or brand content. Watermarks can be text (like a company name or copyright notice) or an image (like a logo). They are commonly used by photographers, designers, e-commerce sellers, and content creators to prevent unauthorized use of their visual assets.
guide.watermark.s1.body2: Watermarks serve multiple purposes: they deter image theft by making unauthorized use less attractive, they help build brand recognition when consistently applied, and they can mark document status (like Draft or Confidential). A well-designed watermark balances visibility with minimal interference to the underlying image content.
guide.watermark.s1.label: What is watermark
guide.watermark.s1.title: What is an Image Watermark and Why Use One
guide.watermark.s2.body1: Text watermarks are the most common and flexible type. You can use any text content such as your name, brand name, website URL, or copyright statement. Our tool lets you customize font size from 8px to 200px, choose any color via a color picker, and adjust opacity from 5% to 100% to achieve the right balance between visibility and subtlety.
guide.watermark.s2.body2: Text watermarks work best for copyright notices (like Copyright 2024 Your Name), brand signatures, social media handles, and status labels (like Sample or Confidential). For best results, choose a color that contrasts with your image background. White text with slight transparency works well on dark images, while dark text works better on light backgrounds.
guide.watermark.s2.label: Text watermark
guide.watermark.s2.title: How to Add Text Watermarks to Images
guide.watermark.s3.body1: Image watermarks let you upload your own logo or graphic file to overlay on your images. This is ideal for businesses that want consistent brand presence across all visual content. Simply upload a PNG with transparency for best results, position it using the 9-point grid or drag it directly on the preview, and adjust size and opacity to complement rather than overwhelm your photos.
guide.watermark.s3.body2: Logo watermarks are particularly effective for product photography, portfolio images, and social media content where brand recognition matters. We recommend using a high-resolution PNG logo with transparent background. Keep the opacity between 60-85% for visibility without distraction, and place it in a corner position so it doesn't obscure important image content.
guide.watermark.s3.label: Logo watermark
guide.watermark.s3.title: How to Add Logo Watermarks to Images
guide.watermark.s4.body1: Tiled watermark mode repeats your watermark text across the entire image surface, creating a pattern that provides comprehensive protection against cropping or screenshot theft. This is especially useful for preview images, sample designs, and confidential documents where you want to ensure the watermark survives any attempt to remove it from a single location.
guide.watermark.s4.body2: In tiled mode, you can control the spacing between repeated watermarks (from 50px to 400px) and the rotation angle (from -180 to 180 degrees). A common anti-theft configuration uses the word SAMPLE at 25% opacity with -30 degree rotation and 150px spacing. This creates a dense but semi-transparent overlay that clearly marks the image as protected while still allowing viewers to see the underlying content.
guide.watermark.s4.label: Tile watermark
guide.watermark.s4.title: How to Use Tiled Watermarks for Full Coverage
guide.watermark.s5.body1: Batch watermarking saves enormous time when you need to protect multiple images with the same settings. Upload up to 20 images at once, configure your watermark once, and apply it to all images with a single click. Each image is processed independently, maintaining its original dimensions and aspect ratio while receiving the identical watermark treatment.
guide.watermark.s5.body2: After batch processing, you can download each image individually or save time by downloading all results as a single ZIP archive. The file names are preserved with a -watermarked suffix added. This workflow is perfect for photographers delivering proof galleries, e-commerce sellers processing product catalogs, and designers preparing portfolio presentations.
guide.watermark.s5.label: Batch watermark
guide.watermark.s5.title: How to Batch Watermark Multiple Images
guide.watermark.s6.body1: You can choose to keep the original image format or convert to JPG, PNG, or WebP during watermarking. JPG offers the smallest file size and widest compatibility but does not support transparency. PNG preserves exact quality and supports transparency but produces larger files. WebP provides the best compression with quality preservation and is ideal for web use.
guide.watermark.s6.body2: When converting to JPG or WebP, you can control the output quality with a slider from 1% to 100%. For most purposes, 85% quality provides an excellent balance between file size and visual fidelity. If you need maximum quality for printing or archival purposes, use PNG format or set quality to 95-100%. The output format and quality settings apply uniformly to all images in a batch.
guide.watermark.s6.label: Format guide
guide.watermark.s6.title: Output Format and Quality Settings
guide.watermark.s7.body1: For copyright protection, place a small text watermark in the bottom-right corner at 60-80% opacity. This is visible enough to assert ownership but unobtrusive enough that viewers can enjoy the image. For brand identity, use a logo watermark in a corner at 70-85% opacity. For anti-theft protection on previews, use tiled mode at 20-30% opacity with a diagonal angle.
guide.watermark.s7.body2: Always preview your watermark on different types of images before batch processing. A watermark that looks good on a dark photo may be invisible on a light one. Test with your lightest and darkest images to ensure consistent visibility. For text watermarks, choose sans-serif fonts at larger sizes for better readability at reduced opacity.
guide.watermark.s7.label: Best practices
guide.watermark.s7.title: Watermark Best Practices for Different Use Cases
guide.watermark.s8.body1: A common mistake is making watermarks too large or too opaque, which distracts from the image itself. Another error is placing watermarks only in corners where they can be easily cropped out. For valuable content, consider tiled mode or placing watermarks near important subject areas. Using low-contrast colors that disappear against similar backgrounds is another frequent issue.
guide.watermark.s8.body2: Avoid using watermarks on images you intend to print at high quality, as any overlay will reduce the final output quality. For social media, remember that some platforms compress images aggressively, which can make fine watermark text blurry or illegible. Always keep an original unwatermarked version of your images stored safely for future use.
guide.watermark.s8.label: Common mistakes
guide.watermark.s8.title: Common Watermark Mistakes to Avoid

guide.rotate.s1.label: What is image rotation
guide.rotate.s1.title: Understanding Image Rotation
guide.rotate.s1.body1: Image rotation changes the orientation of a digital image by turning it around a central point. Common rotations include 90°, 180°, and 270°, as well as arbitrary angles for precise adjustments.
guide.rotate.s1.body2: Unlike simple CSS transforms that only change how an image is displayed, true image rotation re-renders the pixels to create a new image file with the corrected orientation.
guide.rotate.s2.label: Quick rotation
guide.rotate.s2.title: How to Rotate Images 90° Left or Right
guide.rotate.s2.body1: The most common image rotation needs are 90° clockwise (right) and 90° counter-clockwise (left). These are perfect for fixing photos taken in portrait or landscape orientation.
guide.rotate.s2.body2: A 180° rotation turns the image completely upside down, which is useful when a photo was taken with the camera inverted.

guide.rotate.s2.step2.title: Click rotate button

guide.rotate.s2.step3.title: Download result
guide.rotate.s2.step3.desc: Preview the rotated image and download it in your preferred format.
guide.rotate.s3.label: Flip vs rotate
guide.rotate.s3.title: Flip vs Rotate: What is the Difference?
guide.rotate.s3.body1: Rotation turns an image around its center point. Flip creates a mirror image — horizontal flip mirrors left-to-right, while vertical flip mirrors top-to-bottom.
guide.rotate.s3.body2: Flipping is not the same as rotating 180°. A 180° rotation turns the entire image upside down, while a vertical flip creates a mirror reflection across the horizontal axis.
guide.rotate.flip.h.title: Horizontal Flip
guide.rotate.flip.h.desc: Mirrors the image left-to-right. Useful for correcting selfies and creating mirror effects.
guide.rotate.flip.v.title: Vertical Flip
guide.rotate.flip.v.desc: Mirrors the image top-to-bottom. Useful for special compositions and creative effects.
guide.rotate.rotate.title: Rotation
guide.rotate.rotate.desc: Turns the image around its center. 90°/180°/270° and custom angles supported.
guide.rotate.custom.title: Custom Angle
guide.rotate.custom.desc: Enter any angle from -360° to 360° for precise rotation control.
guide.rotate.s4.label: EXIF orientation
guide.rotate.s4.title: What is EXIF Orientation and Why Do Photos Show the Wrong Direction?
guide.rotate.s4.body1: EXIF Orientation is metadata stored in image files that tells software how the image should be displayed. Some cameras and phones save photos in a fixed orientation and rely on EXIF to indicate the correct viewing direction.
guide.rotate.s4.body2: Different software reads EXIF differently, which is why a photo may look correct on your phone but sideways on a computer. Rotating and re-exporting with our tool bakes the correct orientation into the actual pixels.
guide.rotate.s5.label: Custom angles
guide.rotate.s5.title: Using Custom Angle Rotation
guide.rotate.s5.body1: For images that are slightly tilted, custom angle rotation allows precise correction. Enter any value from -360° to 360° to achieve the exact orientation you need.
guide.rotate.s5.body2: Note that non-90° rotations will expand the canvas and may create blank edges. You can choose a background color or keep transparency for PNG/WebP outputs.
guide.rotate.s6.label: Output formats
guide.rotate.s6.title: JPG vs PNG vs WebP After Rotation
guide.rotate.s6.body: JPG is best for photographs but does not support transparency. PNG preserves transparency and is lossless, ideal for designs. WebP offers the smallest file size with transparency support, perfect for web use.
guide.rotate.fmt.jpg: Best for photos. Small file size, no transparency.
guide.rotate.fmt.png: Lossless quality with full transparency support.
guide.rotate.fmt.webp: Smallest file size with transparency. Ideal for web.
guide.rotate.s7.label: Batch rotation
guide.rotate.s7.title: How to Batch Rotate Multiple Images
guide.rotate.s7.body1: You can upload up to 30 images at once and apply the same rotation or flip settings to all of them. This is perfect for organizing photo albums, product images, or screenshots.
guide.rotate.s7.body2: After processing, download all results as a single ZIP file with one click. Each image maintains its original quality while applying your chosen transformation.
guide.rotate.s8.label: Common mistakes
guide.rotate.s8.title: Common Image Rotation Mistakes to Avoid
guide.rotate.s8.body: 1. Rotating JPG multiple times degrades quality due to re-compression. 2. Forgetting to check transparent backgrounds when converting to JPG. 3. Not previewing before batch processing. 4. Ignoring EXIF orientation causing double-rotation issues. 5. Using extreme quality settings unnecessarily.

guide.rotate.howto.desc: A simple 3-step guide to rotate and flip images using our free online tool.
guide.rotate.howto.step1.name: Upload Images
guide.rotate.howto.step1.text: Drag and drop or click to select up to 30 images. Supports JPG, PNG, and WebP formats.
guide.rotate.howto.step2.name: Set Rotation
guide.rotate.howto.step2.text: Choose rotation angle, flip direction, output format, and quality settings.
guide.rotate.howto.step3.name: Download Results
guide.rotate.howto.step3.text: Preview the results and download individually or as a ZIP archive.
seo.rotate_guide.title: Image Rotation Guide - Photo Orientation Fix, 90° Rotate, Flip | ToolboxNova
seo.rotate_guide.description: Learn how to rotate images online. Covers 90° left/right rotation, horizontal/vertical flip, EXIF orientation fix, custom angles, batch rotation, and JPG/PNG/WebP format differences.
seo.rotate_guide.keywords: image rotation guide, rotate photo, fix photo orientation, EXIF orientation, flip image, batch rotate, custom angle rotation
